entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, May 04, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon alert</h2><p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and important decisions after 4 p.m. Chicago time. The moon is in Sagittarius.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today you're bursting with energy to get things done because you're enthusiastic, proactive and ambitious. (Oh yeah.) You might make travel plans. You might also explore opportunities in medicine, the law and higher education. Note: Don't bite off more than you can chew.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're chatty today because Mercury is in your sign, making you eager to share your ideas with others. Furthermore, with the sun also in your sign, you're more confident and enthusiastic! Today you can make headway with behind-the-scenes financial red-tape matters.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a marvelous day to enjoy the company of friends. In particular, you'll be successful dealing with groups and organizations because your enthusiasm and drive will catch their attention. You might even take control. (However, you'll be tactful and charming because Venus is in your sign.)</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Your ambition is aroused today, and you intend to go after what you want. People will step aside, as they do for anyone who knows where they’re going. This is a popular time for you. Many of you are talking to younger people more than usual.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a powerful way to begin your week. The sun at the top of your chart makes you look fabulous in everyone's eyes. Meanwhile, you're gung-ho to travel and explore opportunities in medicine, the law and higher education. Go, go, go!</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a strong day for you! Check out travel plans or act on your desire to expand your world in some way. Meanwhile, today is an excellent day to defend your best interests in discussions about estates, wills, inheritances, shared property, taxes and debt because you have the advantage.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a tricky day because you might attract someone who is very forceful. Meanwhile, you're a people pleaser. (Deal with this combination as best you can, and don't give away the farm.) Explore opportunities to travel and do something different.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today, you begin your week hitting the pavement running. You're gung-ho to get a lot done, which is why not only are you working hard, you're also delegating and allocating as much as possible. "Move that mountain!" "Build that bridge!" (Financial arrangements favor you today.)</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today the moon is in your sign, energized by fiery Mars, making this a wonderful day to socialize. It's also a strong day to work in the entertainment world, the hospitality industry and sports. Decisions about how to share something will be in your favor. (Yay!)</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Although you're keen to play and pursue pleasure this week, including romance, today you're motivated to make some domestic improvements. You might explore redecorating projects or renovations. You might even research a residential change.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Put on your dancing shoes and get out there because your ability to persuade and convince anyone about anything you want is fabulous today. (In fact, you might come on a bit too strong.) This is a productive day for those in sales, marketing, teaching, acting and writing. It’s also a playful day!</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're very enthusiastic about financial matters today or something to do with earnings and cash flow. People notice you (be aware of this). You might make a bid for a raise or act on some moneymaking ideas. You'll enjoy entertaining at home.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Audrey Hepburn (1929-1993) shares your birthday. You are sensible, imaginative, kind and generous. You have a genuine concern for the well-being of others. This is a year of new beginnings, adventures and major changes. Keep your eyes open for new opportunities. Prepare for leadership. What you do now will bring great benefit in the future.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Breaking down Blackhawks' odds entering NHL draft lottery: Shot at Gavin McKenna, Ivar Stenberg on the line
entries.summary: <p>By this point, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> general manager Kyle Davidson has probably memorized the NHL draft-lottery odds for the 31st-place team.</p><p>This marks the third consecutive year the Hawks have finished in that spot, and therefore also marks the third consecutive year they've entered the lottery — Tuesday at 6 p.m. CT — with the exact same odds.</p><p>The Hawks have a 13.5% chance of getting the No. 1 overall pick, a 14.1% chance of getting No. 2, a 30.7% chance of getting No. 3 and a 41.7% chance of getting No. 4, which is the farthest they could fall.</p><p>Considering the Hawks picked No. 1 in 2023, No. 2 in 2024 and No. 3 in 2025, there may be a pattern emerging, but the Hawks obviously hope that trend doesn't hold — and there's no statistical legitimacy to it, of course.</p><p>The last-place Canucks have the highest odds (25.5%) of winning the top selection, while the Rangers (11.5%), Flames (9.5%) and Maple Leafs (8.5%) round out the rest of the top five heading in. The Leafs' stakes are particularly high, because if they fall out of the top five (a 56.2% probability), they'll give their pick to the Bruins.</p><p>Last year's lottery was full of surprises, with the 10th-to-last-place Islanders cashing in on 3.5% odds to land the top pick and the 14th-last Mammoth cashing in on 3.1% odds to get the second pick, so anything can happen.</p><p>For the Hawks, what's at stake is the opportunity to select one of consensus top-two forwards <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/02/23/blackhawks-noticing-gavin-mckenna-growth-2026-nhl-draft-prospects" target="_blank" >Gavin McKenna</a> and Ivar Stenberg, either of whom would likely develop into a stellar addition to their top-six.</p><p>If they slip outside the top two and neither McKenna nor Stenberg falls, they will have to choose between one of several acclaimed defensemen — Keaton Verhoeff, Chase Reid, Carson Carels and Alberts Smits — or fast-rising forward Caleb Malhotra.</p><p>Every prospect option will be analyzed extensively over the next seven weeks leading up to the June 26-27 draft, particularly at the draft combine June 6.</p><h3><b>Panthers’ pick slides</b></h3><p>The Panthers' unexpectedly disastrous, injury-ridden season had one major silver lining for them: It activated the top-10 protection attached to the first-round pick they traded — along with Spencer Knight — to the Hawks for Seth Jones, allowing them to keep it and forcing the Hawks to receive their 2027 first-round pick instead. </p><p>Panthers GM Bill Zito must be thrilled he didn't instead attach top-five protection like the Leafs. The top-10 protection clause wasn't <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/02/06/blackhawks-panthers-nhl-draft-pick-condition-top-10-protected" target="_blank" >publicly discovered</a> until February, creating consternation in the fan base, but both front offices were fully aware of it all along.</p><p>The Panthers enter Tuesday with a 12.4% chance of jumping up into the top three, whereas they can't fall any lower than 10th. They could conceivably trade the pick again, taking advantage of its greater-than-expected value.</p><p>The Hawks, meanwhile, are slated to make only one first-round pick — barring any further trades — for the first time since 2021, but they do own three second-round picks.</p><p>They also already own three 2027 first-round picks: their own, the Panthers' and the Oilers', whose decision to trade such substantial assets for pending free agents Connor Murphy and Jason Dickinson backfired in their first-round playoff loss.</p><p>Davidson could use those three first-rounders as trade chips of his own a year from now. And that Panthers pick is technically now unprotected, even though the Panthers seem more likely than not to surge back into the playoff field next season.</p><p><b>Note: </b>Top Hawks prospect Roman Kantserov's playoff run in Russia ended Sunday, and his contract with his Russian club expires May 31. Negotiations with the Hawks will likely begin soon.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Man fatally shot walking out of Greater Grand Crossing sub restaurant
entries.summary: <p>A man was shot and killed after walking out of a fast food restaurant late Sunday in the Greater Grand Crossing neighborhood on the South Side. </p><p>When the 25-year-old man walked out of Southtown Sub, 112 E. 71st St., around 11:35 p.m., he spotted a gunman standing nearby, according to police and the Cook County medical examiner's office. </p><p>He then heard gunshots and realized the assailant had shot him in the chest and ankle, police said.</p><p>He tried to flee but collapsed and was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he died, police said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Illinois' proposed school cellphone ban doesn't go far enough
entries.summary: <p>Illinois legislators have taken a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/illinois-house/2026/04/17/illinois-house-brings-back-school-cell-phone-ban-legislation" >step forward </a>with <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=2427&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=162470&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >Senate Bill 2427</a>, which bans student <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/02/21/chicago-students-share-mixed-feelings-on-pritzker-proposal-to-restrict-classroom-use-of-cellphones" >cellphone use </a>during the school day. This bill excludes students in independent schools and it only mandates a bell-to-bell ban — from arrival to dismissal — for elementary and middle schoolers, giving high schools the freedom to set more lenient policies. These critical loopholes leave thousands of students unprotected.</p><p>Illinois — one of only a few states having not enacted phone-free-school legislation — lags behind much of the country on this issue, according to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.phonefreeschoolsreport.org/" target="_blank" >Phone-Free Schools State Report Card</a>.</p><p>Excluding independent private schools sends an alarming message to families who expect equal protection under the law. And, allowing high school students to have phones during lunch and breaks does nothing to improve mental health and safety. When they’re only required to put phones away during class, the online world simply waits for them in the hallways. For a student targeted by classmates, unstructured times are often when aggressive cyberbullying occurs.</p><p>My commitment to advocating for meaningful online child safety legislation stems from unimaginable loss. I lost my son <a class="Link" href="https://bucketsoverbullying.org/nates-story/#nates-story" target="_blank" >Nate — forever 15</a> — to suicide after he endured vicious <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/local/2022/04/29/1095683882/illinois-anti-bullying-law-ignored-at-latin-school-parents-say" target="_blank" >cyberbullying</a> by certain basketball teammates and classmates at the Latin School of Chicago, through a basketball group chat, Snapchat posts and a Snapchat message cruelly directing Nate to kill himself.</p><p>Nate reported the chat and posts but appropriate measures were not taken to protect him. The school failed to notify us of Nate’s cyberbullying report, as required by law.</p><p>I am advocating for federal and state online safety reform and fighting to hold schools accountable when they fail to protect students. That’s why I’m deeply concerned about the shortcomings of SB2427.</p><p>The experience of some Illinois communities offers proof that phone bans work. Antioch Upper Grade School now requires students to lock their phones each morning using <a class="Link" href="https://thepioneerwjhs.com/14767/student-life/yondr-pouches-policy-aims-to-keep-students-focused/" target="_blank" >Yondr pouches</a> and unlock them as they leave.</p><p>The results have been transformative, with a dramatic decrease in discipline referrals and behavioral incidents, according to Antioch Elementary District 34 Superintendent Aron Borowiak. When digital noise is removed, students choose to look at one another, talk and engage.</p><p>If Illinois politicians are serious about protecting children from online harm, this law must apply to all Illinois students, bell to bell. Anything less betrays parents and signals that equal protection is optional.</p><p><i>Rose Bronstein, co-founder, </i><a class="Link" href="https://bucketsoverbullying.org/" target="_blank" ><i>Buckets Over Bullying</i></a><i> and </i><a class="Link" href="https://www.techsafelearning.org/" target="_blank" ><i>Tech-Safe Learning Coalition</i></a><i> </i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9b0000" name="module-9b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Speech fit for a king </h3><p>Much like the British Monarch depicted in the 2010 movie, "The King’s Speech," King Charles III delivered a <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/king-charles-iii-us-congress-speech-9ff638ae63a41289dbd9ebfbb550e40e" target="_blank" >brilliant speech</a> before Congress last week. Throughout his address, which was full of humor and a sharp and nuanced understanding of history, Charles demonstrated what a literate leader sounds like — someone with a keen awareness of the symbolism of rhetorical acts and an appreciation of the necessity of finding common ground and reaffirming shared values.</p><p>Charles’ speech came across as considered rather than performed. Musician <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/groups/2593621180772599/posts/4011166265684743/" target="_blank" >Michael Jochum</a> put it well: “There was a quiet gravity to his presence, a kind of composure that didn’t demand attention so much as earn it. His words were measured, deliberate, and carried with them the weight of history without ever feeling heavy-handed.”</p><p>What was especially noteworthy is that Charles — uncharacteristic of speeches made by the head of the British royal family — was able to be political in an eloquent manner. His repudiations of and disagreements with President Donald Trump were clear, forceful and obvious and communicated in a diplomatic manner. We heard the voice of reason and statesmanship: a stark contrast to Trump’s routine out-of-control discourse.</p><p>I agree with <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/groups/2593621180772599/posts/4011166265684743/" target="_blank" >Jochum</a>: “There was something undeniably compelling about witnessing a speaker who understands not only the power of language, but the responsibility that comes with it.”</p><p>As a teacher of speech for more than 40 years, this is my recommendation: If we are to lower the temperature of our public dialogue, reduce hate and eliminate calls for violence in our hyper-polarized world, Charles’ speech should serve as a model.</p><p><i>Richard Cherwitz, professor emeritus, University of Texas at Austin</i></p><h3>No chance Vance</h3><p>Was it just me, or did anyone else notice JD Vance not applauding when King Charles III mentioned <a class="Link" href="https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/28/king-charles-calls-for-nato-unity-ukraine-support-in-us-congress-speech" target="_blank" >supporting Ukraine</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.yahoo.com/news/articles/jd-vance-unexpected-move-king-111203842.html?guccounter=1&guce_referrer=aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZ29vZ2xlLmNvbS8&guce_referrer_sig=AQAAAM13Z29-ixMR8ocuMxyc5cDzPb-OriNFZESRcSxyQcgf8QF0MFbdqVPgdiQ9tf7gPAE2LhG-xe1oZZMDLKgduUsp8zhxhtfLR1TobN2BUnYln-N1V5rHIGPRK2eSxblahmfu-O2BgHhNfMJJmDbL5vU7T5hoJk8T6_CbSN9kI571" target="_blank" >preserving the environment</a>? Ouch! Talk about telegraphing his true feelings.</p><p>This is a man who believes he may actually be Donald Trump's successor. He has no chance. Christians cherish prophecy, so here's a guaranteed one: Vance will definitely not be the next president, and neither his God nor his Savior will be able to make that happen. Anyone who even remotely follows politics knows it's true. Bye-bye No. 2 and good riddance.</p><p><i>Wes Dickson, Orland Park</i></p><h3>Double standards</h3><p>I don’t remember Sun-Times reader <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/05/01/swedish-hospital-shooting-yellow-line-cta-derailment-kimmel-melania-trump" target="_blank" >Barb Marion</a> of Orlando Park writing in and asking President Donald Trump to apologize every time he says hateful things about Democrats, the pope or many others. This is exactly what’s wrong right now with our country. One person can say whatever he wants with no repercussions but the other guy — in this case, Jimmy Kimmel — has to apologize for what he said.</p><p><i>Richard Orrico, Melrose Park</i></p><h3>Marvelous Murakami</h3><h3></h3><p>Move over Shohei Ohtani. There's a new kid in town.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/28/chicago-white-sox-los-angeles-angels-murakami-munetaka-miguel-vargas-tristan-peters-osvaldo-bido-grant-taylor-bryan-hudson-will-venable-rain-delay-dog-day" target="_blank" >Munetaka Murakami</a> has the hearts of Sox fans aflutter as he smashes home runs and rookie records with equal abandon. With only a one-year contract, here's hoping the latest South Side phenom takes a liking to Chicago red hots and deep dish.</p><p>So, how do you say, "it's on the house!" in Japanese?</p><p><i>Bob Ory, Elgin</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Needy friend uses longtime friendship as free therapy
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a friend I have known for a very long time. We bonded 10 years ago over common interests and mutual friends. Over the years, they have suffered from anxiety, depression and OCD. In addition, they are also narcissistic, but it had never been a problem before.</p><p>I have spent many a day and night on the phone with them for hours, letting them talk out their problems. I have always been supportive and empathetic, offering help or suggestions. Abby, they can't hold down a job! They invariably quit for some reason or another, and they have a drinking problem as well.</p><p>It is becoming increasingly difficult to remain friends. After years of being the supportive friend, it seems that's all I am. Whenever this friend wants contact, the entire time is spent talking about them. They never ask how I am doing or what is new with me, and they haven't in years.</p><p>I feel stuck because I don't want this person to feel I am abandoning them. But I'm mentally and emotionally burnt out from constantly trying to talk them down. They claim they go to therapy and take medication, but I can only take their word for it.</p><p>I have tried to be a bit more distant in hopes my friend gets the hint that I can't be available 100% of the time. The last time they reached out, they claimed they thought they scared me. I want to tell them, "I'm not scared, I'm tired." How do I approach this? — <i>SO DONE IN NEW YORK</i></p><p><b>DEAR SO DONE:</b> Here's how: First, understand that this person will continue to use you (instead of a psychotherapist) as often as you allow it. Begin rationing the time you spend on the phone and in person with them. If this "friend" asks again if they are scaring you, tell them what you have told me, that your energy is being sapped and you can no longer spend hours on the phone with them. If you do, you will be doing both of you a favor.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> We were invited to an out-of-town wedding along with some of our children. It cost several hundred dollars, including airfare, hotel, food and gifts. The events started Friday afternoon and lasted until Sunday evening. On the Saturday of that weekend, the bride's family hosted a dinner and informed several of the wedding invitees that they were on their own for that evening. Was that appropriate? Should we say anything to the groom's family with whom we are associated? — <i>OFFENDED IN KANSAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR OFFENDED:</b> The rehearsal dinner is traditionally hosted by the groom's family, but that's not what this Saturday dinner sounds like, since the bride's family hosted it. I would imagine that being told at the last minute to fend for yourselves was both jarring and frustrating, so your feelings are understandable. However, I see nothing positive to be gained at this point by pointing it out to the groom's family, which might only embarrass them.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: A volunteer project is turning Chicagoan’s collection of live recordings into online archive that can play on forever
entries.summary: <p>Aadam Jacobs has been thinking a lot about the late Chicago photographer Vivian Maier.</p><p>“Remember how she took a million photos and after she died, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-culture/2025/09/30/never-seen-photos-acclaimed-vivian-maier-on-auction-oct-7" target="_blank" ><u>they were all found</u></a> … and <i>now </i>she’s famous?” he said about the local nanny/prolific street photographer who never got to see her life’s work celebrated before she passed in 2009. </p><p>“My tapes aren’t going to last forever, and neither am I, and I didn’t want this collection to be discovered long after I die.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Aadam Jacobs started bringing a tape recorder to shows when he was 17 and “it snowballed. At first, it was just a teenage kid who just loved to go to shows, but then it felt like my job to capture everything that was important to me.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Jacobs is talking about his collection of 6,000 cassette and DAT tapes that — not unlike Maier’s unassuming archiving — capture a unique slice of life and culture. In his case, an epoch of live music.</p><p>Starting in 1984 and up until recently, Jacobs brought tape recorders to thousands of indie, rock and punk shows, capturing full audio sets of everyone from R.E.M. at the UIC Pavilion in 1986 to Depeche Mode at Aragon in 1985 to Nirvana (twice) in 1989, once at Metro and once at the now-defunct Club Dreamerz. And the list goes on.</p><p>“I began doing it after learning that I could sneak a tape recorder into concerts from a friend of mine. I was 17 at the time and I’d only just gone to concerts or listened to them on the radio,” he recalls. “And it snowballed. At first, it was just a teenage kid who just loved to go to shows, but then it felt like my job to capture everything that was important to me. It was an obsession.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-700000" name="image-700000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d41e26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Feb%2Fc3dde4c14ef78f11cd996d0db5b7%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c8025a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Feb%2Fc3dde4c14ef78f11cd996d0db5b7%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-12.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bba0cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Feb%2Fc3dde4c14ef78f11cd996d0db5b7%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-12.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="(From left) Brian Emerick and Aadam Jacobs talk in Aadam’s home, Friday, April 24, 2026. | Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bba0cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Feb%2Fc3dde4c14ef78f11cd996d0db5b7%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4020961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Feb%2Fc3dde4c14ef78f11cd996d0db5b7%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-12.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bba0cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2Feb%2Fc3dde4c14ef78f11cd996d0db5b7%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-12.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Once a month, Brian Emerick (left) drives to Aadam Jacobs’ house to pick up about 10 to 15 boxes full of 50 to 100 tapes each, brings them home, rips them to digital files, and then sends them off to a team of about 10 fellow volunteers around the world. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Jacobs’ collection — which up until recently was largely stored in his Hermosa basement — has naturally been a point of curiosity for music fans, especially after the release of a 2023 documentary on Jacobs titled <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/8/23863678/melomaniac-aadam-jacobs-chicago-concert-tapes-documentary-cuff-underground-film-festival" target="_blank" ><u>“Melomaniac,”</u></a> from producers Charles Cotterman and Katlin Schneider. Word trickled into the “tapers” community and soon Jacobs had offers to help digitize the collection for posterity’s sake. With a good chunk of those files now online at <a class="Link" href="https://archive.org/details/aadamjacobs" target="_blank" ><u>Internet Archive</u></a>, and accessible for free, interest has been soaring with the project in the news again.</p><p>“One of the volunteers in our group knew someone at Associated Press<i> </i>and got in contact with them, and they wanted to do an article,” said Brian Emerick of Des Plaines, one of the main coordinators in the project who has also been taping shows since 2002 and found out about Jacobs’ project through the online community board at <a class="Link" href="http://taperssection.com" target="_blank" ><u>Taperssection.com</u></a>. Emerick is the liaison between physical to digital, meaning that once a month, he drives to Jacobs’ house to pick up about 10 to 15 boxes full of 50 to 100 tapes each, brings them home, rips them to digital files, and then sends them off to a team of about 10 fellow volunteers around the world, many of whom also found out about the project on the same forum. They then mix and master the audio, upload it to Internet Archive and add pertinent background details of the who, where and when of each show, much of it pulled from Jacobs’ copious journals.</p><p>Emerick estimates he spends about 60 hours a week on the project, when he’s not working as a database administrator for a law firm. He handles the project with incredible care, including a detailed Excel spreadsheet where he logs everything. “I have about 10,792 rows on it,” he said, breaking down the numbers. “Aadam’s given us about 6,000 tapes, and there’s somewhere in the range of about 1,500 digital files as well. I know a lot of the articles have said there’s 10,000 concerts but it’s really 10,000 sets, two sets a night, two different bands.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-440000" name="image-440000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a18406/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5406x3034+0+285/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F86%2F6e18469c453fb9a88824bb53bff5%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4551979/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5406x3034+0+285/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F86%2F6e18469c453fb9a88824bb53bff5%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-05.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6bd648c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5406x3034+0+285/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F86%2F6e18469c453fb9a88824bb53bff5%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="AADAMJACOBS_2604XX-05.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6bd648c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5406x3034+0+285/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F86%2F6e18469c453fb9a88824bb53bff5%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad54fc3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5406x3034+0+285/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F86%2F6e18469c453fb9a88824bb53bff5%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-05.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6bd648c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5406x3034+0+285/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2F86%2F6e18469c453fb9a88824bb53bff5%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Aadam Jacobs’ collection of 6,000 cassette and DAT tapes that capture an epoch of live music.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Currently, there are 2,500 shows available online with roughly 10 new ones added daily. Emerick teases upcoming entries from James Brown, George Clinton, Jane’s Addiction, Built To Spill, Broken Social Scene and “a lot of emo.”</p><p>While there is still a long way to go, there is also an endpoint. “I haven't recorded anything in about three years,” Jacobs said. “It’s not to say that I won't in the future, but I just don't have that feeling right now. It’s just not fun for me anymore, unfortunately.” Today’s landscape doesn’t help matters — now anyone can record a show with video and slap it onto YouTube, which takes the magic and rarity out of the process.</p><p>Jacobs was unique in his approach, always doing his recordings without any financial gain in mind and “99% aboveboard,” done with the blessings of artists and their teams (as opposed to bootlegs, which are done in secret for a profit). Even so, there were some challenges.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-410000" name="image-410000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04a3b90/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F63%2Feb194f204fdeb4c644f906a6b668%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-04.jpg" alt="Brian Emerick holds a box of cassette tapes featuring live recordings of concerts made by Aadam Jacobs over the course of 39 years, in Aadam’s home in Hermosa, Friday, April 24, 2026. | Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04a3b90/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F63%2Feb194f204fdeb4c644f906a6b668%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18c4252/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F63%2Feb194f204fdeb4c644f906a6b668%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-04.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/04a3b90/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2F63%2Feb194f204fdeb4c644f906a6b668%2Faadamjacobs-2604xx-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Brian Emerick estimates he spends about 60 hours a week on the project. He handles the project with incredible care, including a detailed Excel spreadsheet where he logs everything. “I have about 10,792 rows on it,” he said. “Aadam’s given us about 6,000 tapes, and there’s somewhere in the range of about 1,500 digital files as well.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I got in big trouble with the Metro in 1990 and wasn't allowed in there for five years,” he admits, marking a significant gap in the collection. “I missed Britpop! There’s no Oasis.”</p><p>But there are also great stories, pulled from across the globe, since Jacobs traveled to record shows all over the Midwest, New York, Tennessee, Portland, Oregon, and even as far away as England and New Zealand. “I had a lot of adventures, usually involving me getting there and getting back or finding a place to sleep or whatever,” Jacobs said, remembering a particular moment of riding the bus with Stereolab from Chicago to Minneapolis. Or the time that he was literally the only person at The Nobodies show at Fireside Bowl in 1995.</p><p>“There were a lot of bands like that, a lot of local bands too, where Aadam might’ve caught their only live recording ever,” Emerick said.</p><p>“You could say there was a fear of missing out,” added Jacobs of how they found all these acts. “For most of the ’90s I bought records on a weekly basis or CDs. And I would just go through them all, constantly in search of something new and exciting. A band would put out one 7-inch and decide to come play Chicago, and I'd show up with my equipment and they'd be like, how do you even know who we are?”</p><p>That curiosity still lives in him today. Jacobs makes a living selling records at record shows and through consignment at High Voltage in Rogers Park, estimating he has an inventory of 12,000 vinyl albums in addition to a personal collection of 15,000. And he’ll have more special titles to add soon. Jacobs is working with an unnamed label for a full-scale physical release of selections from his recorded archives. Though he can’t reveal too much yet, he said, “details will follow.” That, too, goes back to the same mission: wanting to share the wealth with fellow music fans.</p><p>“I figured I might as well allow these tapes to be heard by everyone who wants to hear them because they’ve just been sitting in my home all these years doing nobody any good,” he said. “Now I‘m able to make a lot of people happy. There have been so many who have told me, ‘I’m so glad this exists.’”</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Is it dance? Is it art? Brendan Fernandes defies definition
entries.summary: <p>The Murphy Auditorium inside Chicago’s Driehaus Museum is an unusual room with a lot of churchy touches. Choir stalls line the back wall, a pipe organ console sits parked at the side of the balcony, and a large stained-glass window identifies the space’s former owner, the American College of Surgeons.</p><p>The artist Brendan Fernandes first saw the space during its renovation. The Driehaus’s executive director, Lisa Key, showed it to him with a new-toy glee — the museum had recently acquired the building that houses it. Fernandes read the hint and asked if he could make something here.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" class="Form-newsletter-wrapper" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>“She was like, ‘What are you thinking?’” Fernandes recalled. “And I was like, ‘I’m thinking a lot.’”</p><p>Although couched modestly, “a lot” neatly summarizes what Fernandes does. He brings many arts to his art, including dance, visual arts, design and textiles, and he collaborates with other artists and performers across genres, making him a popular name this month as the city’s largest art fair and several satellite events unfold across the city. </p><p>He is a subverter of forms, determined to work across and through any restrictions that might bind him or categories that might bound him. Unbound and unbounded, Fernandes is in the broadest sense an artist.</p><p>Take his work unfolding at the Driehaus, where he is now artist in residence. The residency takes the name “In the Round,” referring — typically — to many things at once, such as theater in the round, where the audience surrounds the actors, and sculpture in the round, where a piece of visual art is intended to be viewable from all 360 degrees of perspective.</p><hr><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure alt=""In the Round" at the Driehaus Museum" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11c52b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5106x2866+0+2397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F90%2Fd2a5e4e24acca9b5f7c998890276%2F030-bob00254-web-v1.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8c94184/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5106x2866+0+2397/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F90%2Fd2a5e4e24acca9b5f7c998890276%2F030-bob00254-web-v1.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/11c52b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5106x2866+0+2397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F80%2F90%2Fd2a5e4e24acca9b5f7c998890276%2F030-bob00254-web-v1.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dancers leave handprints, footprints and humidity marks on the surfaces of 12 mirrored benches arranged in a dodecagon.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Bob</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><hr><p>Periodically from this week through November, the space will be prepped for performances of “Score for the Murphy Auditorium.” At a recent preview, seven dancers performed semi-improvisatory contemporary dance, part of which followed a flocking pattern, where a lead dancer created a short series of steps that the others learned and mimicked, and then the lead rotated to another dancer.</p><p>The dancers danced around 12 mirrored benches arranged in a dodecagon, designed to occupy the center of the auditorium’s main level. They left handprints, footprints and humidity marks on the surfaces, prompting murmurs in the audience about whether there’s a Windex budget. The dancers sometimes wielded quilted textiles, and original music was piped in alongside.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-420000" name="module-420000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>Brendan Fernandes: Score for the Murphy Auditorium</b></h3><b>Where</b>: Driehaus Museum, 50 E. Erie St.<br><b>When</b>: May 6 and periodically through November<br><b>Ticket info</b>: Included with museum admission, free–$23. Tickets at <a class="Link" href="https://driehausmuseum.org/" target="_blank" >driehausmuseum.org</a>.</div> </div><p>Although few artists work in this hyperintersectional way, for Fernandes, 46, it feels more like a continuation of the childhood sense of curiosity and experimentation. “As a young kid, I was always dancing and drawing and painting,” he said. He was born in Nairobi, Kenya, and moved while still a child to the suburbs of Toronto. His high school offered extensive arts opportunities, and he studied ballet.</p><p>Doing his BFA at York University in Toronto, he did both visual arts and dance. “I was always told you can’t do both. You have to pick one or the other.”</p><p>A hamstring injury meant he really couldn’t do both. For an MFA program at the University of Western Ontario, he practiced only visual art. But then post-MFA he enrolled in the Whitney Museum of American Art’s famously cross-disciplinary Independent Study Program, where he realized the falseness of the binary of visual art on one side and dance on the other.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0a0000" name="image-0a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f517419/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F95%2F0649a8a74f7aa25e4dd602ddad8e%2Fbrendanfernandes-260504-14.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b4140f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F95%2F0649a8a74f7aa25e4dd602ddad8e%2Fbrendanfernandes-260504-14.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c1dab53/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F95%2F0649a8a74f7aa25e4dd602ddad8e%2Fbrendanfernandes-260504-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Brendan Fernandes at Driehaus Museum at 50 East Erie in River North, Friday, April 17, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c1dab53/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F95%2F0649a8a74f7aa25e4dd602ddad8e%2Fbrendanfernandes-260504-14.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/571fc32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F95%2F0649a8a74f7aa25e4dd602ddad8e%2Fbrendanfernandes-260504-14.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c1dab53/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2f%2F95%2F0649a8a74f7aa25e4dd602ddad8e%2Fbrendanfernandes-260504-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Brendan Fernandes brings many arts to his art, including dance, visual arts, design and textiles.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>He cites “Encomium” as a breakthrough work for shattering this binary. In it, Fernandes loosely choreographed instructions for two male dancers to assume poses they hold until they cannot any longer. The instructions quote Phaedrus’s speech on love in Plato’s “Symposium,” creating a symbolic register about Fernandes’s relationship to dance.<i> </i>“It was sort of like a breakup letter to myself,” he said.</p><p>In Chicago, where he is now based, he continued to play with the idea. In “72 Seasons<i>,” </i>created for the Lurie Garden in Millennium Park in 2021 during the COVID-19 pandemic, Fernandes placed ballet dancers in the gardens and cued their steps with placards embedded in the soil like plant identifiers. The choreography was inspired by the Japanese calendar of microseasons.</p><p>“There were people coming to the garden, not expecting a contemporary art or dance experience, but stumbling upon these cues,” said Stephanie Cristello, a curator who works often with Fernandes. “It has been a constant of our collaborations together — how can we infuse these small unexpected moments of dance or contemporary art into the everyday?”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-900000" name="image-900000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d939496/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+108/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F7d%2Ffdaa55c34ff68543c39b7de53f1e%2Fdriehausxbrendan-mreidphotog-116.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a68e5a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+108/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F7d%2Ffdaa55c34ff68543c39b7de53f1e%2Fdriehausxbrendan-mreidphotog-116.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbd8e8e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+108/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F7d%2Ffdaa55c34ff68543c39b7de53f1e%2Fdriehausxbrendan-mreidphotog-116.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A performance of Brendan Fernandes's "In the Round" at the Driehaus Museum." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbd8e8e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+108/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F7d%2Ffdaa55c34ff68543c39b7de53f1e%2Fdriehausxbrendan-mreidphotog-116.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8c968c3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+108/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F7d%2Ffdaa55c34ff68543c39b7de53f1e%2Fdriehausxbrendan-mreidphotog-116.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbd8e8e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2048x1149+0+108/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F7d%2Ffdaa55c34ff68543c39b7de53f1e%2Fdriehausxbrendan-mreidphotog-116.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“It has been a constant of our collaborations together,” said Stephanie Cristello, a curator who works often with Fernandes, “how can we infuse these small unexpected moments of dance or contemporary art into the everyday?”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michelle Reid</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Cristello is also the curator for In the Round. She said the performance-installation in the Murphy Auditorium blurs these boundaries as well, not hewing to the conventions of a dance show — the audience doesn’t come at a designated time, doesn’t sit in a seat, and doesn’t see a beginning, middle and end. “You’re walking into essentially a moving sculpture,” she said.</p><p>“I call this thing queering architecture or queering space,” Fernandes said. “So within the moniker of queer — outside of gender and sexuality binaries — it’s this idea of being a nebulous kind of entity that is constantly in flux and in change that is self-evolving. It’s always becoming something else.”</p><p>As Fernandes’s residence unspools, museum visitors during the active weeks in May, September, October and November can visit the space even outside of the performance times and potentially see rehearsal or projects produced by subsets of the dance corps, a cadre of freelancers Fernandes has worked with several times.</p><p>And because of the improvisatory elements, it’s always different. Or put another way, it’s never totally bounded.</p>
entries.author: Graham Meyer | WBEZ

entries.title: Little-known court case hints at larger issue in Orthodox Jewish community — child sexual abuse, and secrecy
entries.summary: <p>At first blush, a case unfolding in Cook County Judge Daniel Gallagher’s courtroom has the hallmarks of a neighbor dispute that’s taken an ugly turn.</p><p>Two men living in the same North Side neighborhood are at odds.</p><p>Threatening words may have been exchanged between the men, or their associates.</p><p>An armed bodyguard was hired for one of the men and is accused of slashing one of the other man’s tires.</p><p>The main participants want a “no stalking” order against each other, which Gallagher eventually may rule on.</p><p>But there are deep undercurrents in the case that speak to how the Orthodox Jewish community the men are part of deal with allegations of child sexual abuse — which often is shrouded in secrecy, with the offenders at times seemingly shown deference over victims.</p><p>One of the men involved in the court case, Michael Weldler, has alleged that he was sexually abused years ago as a boy at a Jewish school on the North Side by an older student employed there as a book binder.</p><p>The other man in the case in front of Gallagher, David Sussman, was accused in a lawsuit of sexually assaulting his roommate at a Jewish boarding school in Long Beach, New York, when they were teens, also years ago.</p><p>Sussman, who is now 38 and a rabbi, and Weldler, 27, don’t know each other.</p><p>But when Sussman moved to Chicago within the last few years to take a teaching job at another Jewish school called Yeshiva Eitz Chaim, Weldler heard about the allegations and was stunned.</p><p>Weldler reached out to one or more rabbis he knew who were running the school, which is located on the edge of the Sauganash and Peterson Park neighborhoods, and relayed concerns.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fd0000" name="image-fd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6fc3b2c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F95%2F28%2F4aa40525404e8ad6b4b302ec5d96%2Fherguth.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Michael Weldler.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Unhappy that school leaders apparently didn’t plan any drastic action on Sussman, Weldler made it clear he may distribute leaflets in the community about him.</p><p>Court records show that one of the rabbis passed along what Weldler contends was a threat to him through Weldler’s father.</p><p>Weldler is accused in court records of verbally confronting Sussman in the neighborhood, including once while he was walking home with a colleague and Weldler allegedly told him: “Does your family know what you did? Did you tell them yet?”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/06/rabbi-accused-sex-abuse-boarding-school-now-teacher-yeshiva-eitz-chaim-chicago" >A rabbi accused of sexually abusing roommate as a teen is now teaching at Orthodox Jewish school in Chicago</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>At some point, Sussman’s father hired his son an armed guard, who Weldler has accused of saying something to the effect of, “If I ever see you walking around here again, I will break every single bone in your body,” court records show.</p><p>The guard was accused of slashing one of Weldler’s tires.</p><p>Weldler was accused of planting leaflets citing the abuse allegations against Sussman on the windshield of his car while it was parked in front of his home.</p><p>Both sides deny doing anything threatening or illegal.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-900000" name="image-900000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad735f7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1568x880+0+56/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2Fae%2Ff6a56e164c44b4bbf0024db97d33%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-30-at-2-57-33-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/265656f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1568x880+0+56/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2Fae%2Ff6a56e164c44b4bbf0024db97d33%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-30-at-2-57-33-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2260cc6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1568x880+0+56/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2Fae%2Ff6a56e164c44b4bbf0024db97d33%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-30-at-2-57-33-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Some of the allegations contained in a lawsuit last year against Rabbi David Sussman and a Jewish school in New York where, as a teenager, he was accused of sexually assaulting his high school dormitory roommate. Both are identified under pseudonyms in the case." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2260cc6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1568x880+0+56/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2Fae%2Ff6a56e164c44b4bbf0024db97d33%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-30-at-2-57-33-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47a6fa8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1568x880+0+56/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2Fae%2Ff6a56e164c44b4bbf0024db97d33%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-30-at-2-57-33-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2260cc6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1568x880+0+56/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2Fae%2Ff6a56e164c44b4bbf0024db97d33%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-30-at-2-57-33-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Some of the allegations contained in a lawsuit last year against Rabbi David Sussman and a Jewish school in New York where, as a teenager, he was accused of sexually assaulting his high school dormitory roommate. Both are identified under pseudonyms in the case.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Adam Sheppard, an attorney representing Weldler, describes child sex abuse allegations as “obviously an impetus for the dispute.”</p><p>But Sheppard calls the pending case in front of Gallagher “a classic intersection of the First Amendment and the law and we believe” Weldler “expressed his First Amendment rights within the law.”</p><p>Sussman’s attorney Hal M. Garfinkel asserted in court: “This is not a case about fliers. This is a case about an individual... who terrorized the Orthodox Jewish community. Mr. Weldler was under the misconception, the mistaken belief, that my client, David Sussman, was a child molester.”</p><p>Sussman has not been charged with a crime.</p><p>But a 2021 lawsuit filed by his former boarding school roommate says Sussman repeatedly sexually assaulted him, while the New York school did nothing to protect him.</p><p>The case was settled for undisclosed terms last year, and the case was sealed.</p><p>Before that occurred, a 2019 letter between Sussman and his accuser was entered into the court record in which Sussman wrote: “I would like to apologize for what happened when we were together in 11th grade. As I got older, I realized the severity of what I’ve done. My words cannot undo my actions, but I would like to express my deepest regret and apology.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The area where one of the confrontations last year between the men involved in the case in front of Cook County Judge Daniel Gallagher occurred.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Robert Herguth / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Typically, such settlements come without an admission of wrongdoing by either side.</p><p>It’s unclear when Yeshiva Eitz Chaim first learned about the allegations against Sussman. But Garfinkel previously said: “Despite the civil claim being unsubstantiated, the School, out of an abundance of caution for the safety of its students, sought legal, rabbinical and professional guidance as to the appropriate next steps.”</p><p>“After doing extensive independent research, they unanimously advised the School leadership that there is no cause for concern. The School proceeded to notify parents, lay leaders and supporters as to the civil suit along with the School’s reaction and subsequent decision. They all wholeheartedly supported the School in its handling of the situation.”</p><p>Garfinkel also represented a member of Chicago’s Orthodox community, Avi Levin, who was accused by Weldler of sexually abusing him years ago. Levin was charged criminally with the abuse, but he denied misconduct and the case was dismissed by prosecutors before it went to trial.</p><p>A lawsuit filed by Weldler against Levin, and the school that employed him, is pending in Cook County.</p><p>Yet even with some or all of this known in the community, Levin appears to have remained part of the Jewish community’s volunteer security service — giving him an air of respectability, and free movement in a community teeming with children, according to victim advocates.</p><p>In another case last year, several members of Chicago’s Orthodox community — including a rabbi who runs a Jewish school in West Rogers Park — rallied behind former resident<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/09/30/north-side-rabbi-jewish-school-yeshiva-ohr-boruch-veitzener-cheder-west-rogers-park-dovid-akiva-shenkman" > Dovid Akiva Shenkman </a>when he was convicted on child pornography charges.</p><p>They portrayed his crimes as victimless and asked the judge for leniency.</p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Some Brookfield Zoo staffers strike after negotiations fail
entries.summary: <p>Dozens of of Brookfield Zoo Chicago union workers wanted their voices to be heard as they stood outside the main entrance with pickets in hand Monday morning on the first day of an authorized strike after prior contract negotiations were unsuccessful. </p><p>Several dozen grounds crew members and union representatives of the zoo, 3300 Golf Rd. in Brookfield were among those striking, with over 100 participants expected by the end of the day. </p><p>Grasping a sign reading “We’re not lion about being understaffed” was Carrie Sapienza, a senior groundskeeper and equipment operator who has worked at the zoo for over three decades and wants her voice heard.</p><p>"We have staff that worked here 25, 35, 45 years. We're dedicated to these animals," said Sapienza, who is also the chief union steward for local 727. "This is not a job, it's a career. We've invested our lives into this place. It's a second home and we want to be recognized for that."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-aa0000" name="image-aa0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
