1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0c2871d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+76/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F4c%2Fc9f9b9524579af7690d62116f50d%2Fzoo-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb947c6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+76/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0f%2F4c%2Fc9f9b9524579af7690d62116f50d%2Fzoo-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Members of the Chicago Zoological Society teamsters picket outside the south gate of the Brookfield Zoo at 3301 Golf Rd. in Riverside, Monday, May 4, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p><i>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</i></p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a released statement Monday morning, zoo officials said they were "disappointed but anticipated" the strike and have plans in place to ensure continued operations. </p><p>"While the Zoo continued bargaining in good faith as recently as Friday, we are disappointed that an agreement was not reached," zoo officials said. "A strike is neither necessary nor inevitable, and our focus remains on reaching a fair and responsible contract—one that supports our employees while protecting the Zoo’s long-term sustainability and mission."</p><p>The most notable areas union employees are looking to address include under staffing, healthcare and wages that match increased living costs. </p><p>Union leadership say the zoo hasn't been negotiating in good faith and that officials "came back to the table with no movement" after the latest round of stalled negotiations Friday. </p><p>"We want the zoo to come to the table and bargain in good faith, to stop violating the law and treat these members with the dignity and respect they deserve," said Caleen Carter-Patton, president and business manager of Teamsters Local 727. </p><p>Carter-Patton urged the public to stay away from the zoo as the strike progresses. She noted that while operations at the zoo will continue, guests won't get the same atmosphere the zoo is known for.</p><p>"You're not going to get that world class experience people are used to," Carter-Patton said.<br></p>

entries.title: Hospital shooting reveals security fails
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: The April 25 shooting that killed a police officer and gravely wounded another was the second shooting at an Endeavor Health hospital in less than a year. We look at potential security fail points.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: An escalating North Side dispute spurred by sex abuse allegations, a new bar's community donation model and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-j/" target="_blank" >The Cubs beat the Diamondbacks</a>, 8-4; <a class="Link" Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-h/" target="_blank" >Swedish Hospital shooting of police highlights critical security fail points</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-k/" target="_blank" >Kade Heather</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-u/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón</a></h5><p><b>Two shootings</b>: A gun brought into Endeavor Health Swedish Hospital and used in a shooting that killed a Chicago police officer and left another gravely wounded exposes potential security fail points when an arrestee arrives for treatment. The April 25 shooting was the second at an Endeavor Health hospital in the Chicago area in less than a year.</p><p><b>Key contex</b>t: Last summer, a man who had been taken to Evanston Hospital for a psychiatric evaluation reached for a gun in his backpack after he became agitated, striking a security guard trying to restrain him, according to prosecutors. In the April incident, prosecutors accuse Alphanso Talley of using a 10mm handgun to kill Chicago cop John Bartholomew and wound his partner at Swedish Hospital.</p><p><b>Potential fail points</b>: It’s unclear what type of search officers conducted after Talley’s initial arrest. It's also unclear if anyone, police or hospital security, patted down Talley again once he was at the hospital. Endeavor Health, which owns Swedish Hospital, previously said Talley "was wanded upon arrival" as part of hospital safety protocols. Security experts say a wand used correctly should have picked up the gun, even if it was concealed on the body.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>THE >Court case hints at larger issue in Orthodox Jewish community: Child sex abuse and secrecy</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-n/" target="_blank" >Robert Herguth</a></h5><p><b>Zooming in</b>: In a case unfolding before Cook County Judge Daniel Gallagher, it seems on the surface that two men are involved in a neighbor dispute, with threatening words, slashed tires and alleged stalking. Each man wants an order of protection against the other. But deep undercurrents speak to how the Orthodox Jewish community deals with allegations of child sexual abuse, which often is shrouded in secrecy while offenders at times are seemingly shown deference over victims.</p><p><b>Who's involved</b>: Michael Weldler claims he was sexually abused as a boy at a Jewish school on the North Side. The other man in the case, David Sussman, was accused in a lawsuit of sexually assaulting his roommate at a Jewish boarding school in New York years ago. Sussman, now a rabbi, and Weldler don’t know each other. But when Sussman moved to Chicago to take a teaching job at Yeshiva Eitz Chaim, Weldler heard about the accusations and allegedly tried to make his concerns about Sussman known.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FOOD SCENE 🍽️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-x/" target="_blank" >New bar tries loyalty program to raise funds ahead of opening</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-m/" target="_blank" >Maggie Hennessy</a></h5><h5></h5><p><b>Community donation model</b>: Jeremy Leven and Ines de Haro are hoping Chicagoans will buy into their new bar, Gilda, after launching an experimental loyalty program to raise capital ahead of their June 1 opening.</p><p><b>How it works</b>: Customers who donate any amount will receive a "Compañero Card," which is good for a percentage off their bill every time they dine at Gilda until their full contribution is "paid back.” Up to $499 gets them 8% off; $500 to $999, 10%; and $1,000, 12%. The bigger the donation, the more Gilda will "give back," not just in discounts but also in preferential treatment for reservations and first dibs on goings-on at the restaurant.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The experiment will be closely watched in an era that increasingly favors the corporate-backed and the deep-pocketed, and in a year when several local independent restaurateurs have closed due to shaky industry economics.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-c/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Courtney B. is the future owner of a Habitat for Humanity house.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-tl/" target="_blank" >Austin resident builds her first home alongside Habitat for Humanity</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-tr/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a></h5><p>Courtney B. has only ever called Austin home. But as housing costs rose and her options began to shrink, she feared being priced out. For the past eight years, Courtney rented a third-floor apartment, where long trips up the stairs and limited space often made it difficult to host family.</p><p>Now, with the help of Habitat for Humanity Chicago, the 45-year-old is helping to build the first home she’ll ever own.</p><p>On Saturday morning, she joined volunteers from Habitat Chicago and members of By the Hand Club for Kids, an after-school program serving children across the city, to paint the walls of the house she is working to finish.</p><p>Next month, she will move into the home — a 1,800-square-foot, four-bedroom, two-bathroom house with a fenced-in backyard and detached two-car garage.</p><p>"Is this really happening? Am I really going to have my own house?" Courtney said in excitement as she stood painting drywall.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-ty/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>What's the best household item you've scored in a Chicago alley? Do you still have it in your home today?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Email your answer to us</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><p>On Friday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-tt/" target="_blank" >What’s your defining memory of going to the Lincolnwood Town Center?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"Buying a new suit at Carsons for an interview." — <i>Ray Bieniasz</i></p><p>"I took my son there in 1996 for his first visit [and] picture with the Easter Bunny." — <i>Kimberly Rose</i></p><p>"Somehow, my friend convinced me to go with him there to get his mother a present on Christmas Eve. Pure shopping hell." — <i>Jeffrey A. Janusch</i></p><p>"Running from one bus at the end of the mall to the other [end] to catch the other bus, just to see it pull away and then slowly drive around the parking lot as you watch it, hopelessly looking at the next bus arrival. So you go back inside the mall, only to lose track of time and miss the second bus!" — <i>Tony E. Meneses</i></p><p>"Getting my ears pierced at Claire's as a little girl and then many years later, taking my oldest son to trick-or-treat there as a toddler when it snowed one Halloween! So many great memories. I'm very sad to see it go." — <i>Amanda Grace Marcheschi</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdjtyky-l-ti/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Sky sign point guard Natasha Cloud
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> signed veteran point guard Natasha Cloud, the team announced Monday. The Athletic reported the deal is for one year and worth $550,000.</p><p>"Natasha is one of the best passers and defenders in our league," general manager Jeff Pagliocca said in a statement. "She fits in with the other proven winners on our roster."</p><p>Cloud averaged 10.1 points, 5.1 assists and 1.2 steals per game for the Liberty last season. In 10 seasons, she has earned three All-Defensive team selections and won a championship with the Mystics in 2019.</p><p>But weeks into training camp, she remained unsigned, one of the curiosities of the condensed free agency period. Cloud has not spoken publicly about why.</p><p>Liberty general manager Jonathan Kolb vouched for her at his preseason press conference, calling her a "phenomenal teammate" and an "awesome human being."</p><p>Cloud wrote on Threads that same day: "I have yet to speak. That's intentional & my power. I've just worked. No sulking, no whining. Work. & I will continue to work as I always have. Proving myself right year, after year, after year. (11) to be exact. I know who I am. I know my value/worth. & ain't nothing traditional."</p><p>Cloud will upgrade the Sky's defense and help shoulder point guard responsibilities while veteran Courtney Vandersloot continues to rehab an ACL injury.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Chicago launches e-scooter safety campaign in wake of recent scooter deaths
entries.summary: <p>Chicago is launching a safety campaign for e-scooters in the wake of expected record ridership and recent scooter-related deaths. </p><p>The campaign, spearheaded by Mayor Brandon Johnson and the Chicago Department of Transportation, aims to improve rider behavior, reduce sidewalk riding and strengthen parking compliance ahead of peak riding season, according to a city press release. The campaign includes new rider safety messaging across the city, expanded scooter parking and bike infrastructure, and stronger compliance requirements for operators and riders that will include stronger parking standards and expanded sidewalk riding detection technology.</p><p>"E-scooters are an increasingly popular way for Chicagoans to get around, and as ridership grows, so does our responsibility to make sure our streets remain safe and accessible for everyone," Johnson said in a press release. "We're bringing together education, infrastructure, and accountability to support safer riding, reduce conflicts on sidewalks, and ensure shared mobility works for all Chicagoans.”</p><p>More than 200 new Divvy stations are planned citywide to improve parking options and more than 100 miles of bikeways have been added since 2023, officials said. </p><p>“Divvy and Lime scooters are an important part of Chicago’s transportation network, and our responsibility is to make sure that system works safely and effectively for everyone,” said CDOT Acting Commissioner William Cheaks Jr.<b> </b>“This campaign responds to feedback we’ve heard from residents and community stakeholders and reflects our commitment to safer streets, stronger accountability, and treating shared micromobility as essential transportation infrastructure.”</p><p>So far, more than 1 million Lime and Divvy e-scooter trips have been taken throughout Chicago in 2026, city officials said. Last year, more than 7 million trips were taken — a record in e-scooter usage for the city. </p><p>At least two trips taken on e-scooters have ended tragically this year. 17-year-old <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/05/01/loved-ones-launch-fundraiser-for-venezuelan-teenager-killed-in-south-side-scooter-crash" target="_blank" >Astrid Alexandra Carrillo Noguera</a> and 15-year-old <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/girl-15-killed-while-riding-scooter-in-south-shore-hit-and-run" target="_blank" >Violet Harris</a> were both killed in accidents roughly a month apart on the South Side earlier this spring. Both were struck by vehicles while riding e-scooters.</p><p>Critics have voiced safety concerns over the e-scooters as riders faced injuries varying from bumps and bruises to life-altering — and sometimes fatal — injuries. </p><p>With no official citywide tally of e-scooter related deaths and injuries, advocates noted a combination of responsibility from riders and e-scooter organizations and enforcement from police would help prevent crashes. </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2024/10/09/lime-divvy-chicago-e-scooter-riders-serious-injuries" >Chicago e-scooter riders face serious injuries, and no one’s tracking how often they’re hurt</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: En el Día Internacional del Trabajador, madre de Chicago lleva a su familia a la protesta
entries.summary: <p>En casa, la mañana del viernes, Flor Ramírez se movía entre tareas y preparativos para la marcha del Primero de mayo por la tarde: empacando tamales y limpiando la camioneta familiar para asegurarse de que hubiera suficiente espacio para los niños y para un amigo que planeaba recoger. Yessari, su hija de 8 años, dijo que le gustaba especialmente ver a su madre idear cómo se iban a vestir para la manifestación.</p><p>El año pasado, Flor Ramírez hizo mariposas para que Yessari y su hermana Saori las llevaran en la cabeza. “Las mariposas simbolizan la libertad”, dijo Yessari. Este año, utilizando una plantilla y brillo, su madre pintó “ICE OUT” en las mejillas de la niña, una palabra en cada mejilla.</p><p>Flor Ramírez no siempre pudo unirse a las marchas y manifestaciones anuales del Día de Mayo (May Day) que se celebran el 1 de mayo, un día reconocido en todo el mundo en defensa de los derechos de los trabajadores y en Chicago por los derechos de los inmigrantes también.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3a0000" name="module-3a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>En 2006, Ramírez, que había llegado recientemente a los Estados Unidos de Puebla, México, quería unirse a la marcha del Primero de mayo por los derechos de los inmigrantes, pero no conocía a nadie que pudiera llevarla. Tenía 18 años y trabajaba en un Chipotle en Niles, IL.</p><p>Ramírez dijo que el gerente de Chipotle se rió cuando le pidió el día libre. Le dijo que había otras formas de ayudar. Pero Ramírez quería ir.</p><p>“Está bien”, recordó que él dijo. “Ve, pero no vuelvas”.</p><p>En ese entonces, dijo, solía escuchar al locutor Rafael Pulido, “<a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2006/06/15/5488198/the-power-of-spanish-language-radio" target="_blank" >El Pistolero</a>” en la radio, que en su programa instaba a los trabajadores y familias inmigrantes a presentarse por sus derechos a medida que crecía el debate sobre un proyecto de ley federal que habría criminalizado a los inmigrantes sin estatus legal. Ahora, casi dos décadas después, Flor Ramírez, junto con su esposo, Armando, y sus hijos de 16 años, Uri, y sus hijas Saori, de 10, y Yessari, de 8, asistieron a la marcha y manifestación del Primero de mayo en Union Park.</p><p>Hace 20 años, la amenaza era un proyecto de ley que habría criminalizado a los inmigrantes sin estatus legal. Flor Ramírez dice que la amenaza ahora se siente mayor, ya que incluso las personas con estatus legal, residencia o ciudadanía están en riesgo.</p><p>Como muchas familias inmigrantes, la familia Ramírez está dividida en cuanto a estatus legal y experiencias: algunos de ellos nacieron en México, otros en los Estados Unidos; algunos tienen permisos de trabajo o residencia, o son ciudadanos estadounidenses.</p><p>Flor y su esposo, Armando, no tuvieron estatus legal durante años antes de recibir protección temporal contra la deportación y autorización para trabajar a través de la Acción Diferida para la Ejecución Laboral, o <a class="Link" href="https://immpolicytracking.org/policies/reported-uscis-ends-program-to-shield-workers-reporting-abuse/" target="_blank" >DALE</a>, un proceso de la era Biden de 2023 creado para los trabajadores inmigrantes involucrados en investigaciones de violaciones laborales. El futuro del programa ahora es incierto. La administración de Trump detuvo el procesamiento de las solicitudes de DALE el año pasado, aunque los trabajadores que ya recibieron acción diferida pueden seguir protegidos hasta que sus protecciones expiren.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-382ffc" name="image-382ffc"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3e2c422/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F33%2Fbc%2Fa9d142f74559a4086b2f2c919045%2F186a0428.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/18d5477/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F33%2Fbc%2Fa9d142f74559a4086b2f2c919045%2F186a0428.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a5e09d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F33%2Fbc%2Fa9d142f74559a4086b2f2c919045%2F186a0428.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Flor Ramirez lidera un canto en la manifestación del Primero de Mayo." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a5e09d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F33%2Fbc%2Fa9d142f74559a4086b2f2c919045%2F186a0428.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c6b624d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F33%2Fbc%2Fa9d142f74559a4086b2f2c919045%2F186a0428.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a5e09d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F33%2Fbc%2Fa9d142f74559a4086b2f2c919045%2F186a0428.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Flor Ramirez lidera a los manifestantes en un canto durante la marcha del Día del Trabajo que comenzó en Union Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La familia Ramírez llegó a la marcha del Día de Mayo del viernes lista para hacer mucho ruido. Armando había fabricado tambores caseros con cubetas que compró en Home Depot. Las niñas golpeaban unos panderos mientras marchaban por Union Park. La música sonaba fuerte mientras la tarde se enfriaba y las niñas se envolvían en cobijas dentro de un carro que habían traído por si se cansaban.</p><p>Estar en la manifestación del Primero de mayo junto a toda la familia se siente especialmente importante para Ramírez este año debido a lo que su familia experimentó durante el Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>En otoño, su hijo Uri, que es ciudadano estadounidense, estaba esperando que lo recogieran de la escuela cuando recibió un mensaje de texto de su hermano mayor informando que agentes federales de inmigración estaban en una gasolinera cerca de su escuela. Así que, en lugar de salir por la salida habitual, Uri salió por otra diferente, esperando que los agentes no lo vieran.</p><p>Flor luego le dijo que no tenía nada de qué preocuparse.</p><p>“Mamá”, le dijo, “¿has visto mi cara?”.</p><p>“Nací aquí, pero… todavía sentía un poco de miedo”, contó Uri. “Todavía sentía que me llevarían lejos de mi familia, mis amigos, mi propio hogar”.</p><p>Para las personas que preguntan ‘¿cuál es el propósito de marchar en el Primero de mayo? ¿Qué se ha logrado?”, Flor Ramírez tiene una respuesta: “Nunca he escuchado que algún derecho se haya ganado estando en casa o desde debajo de la cama”, dijo. Esa es la lección que quiere transmitir a sus hijos: que el silencio no los protegerá.</p><p>Si sus hijos aprenden algo de marchar en el Primero de mayo, ella quiere que sea la creencia de que pertenecen aquí, que no tienen que hacerse pequeños para estar seguros y que no están solos.</p><p>“Incluso si no nos quieren, nuestras raíces ya están aquí. Ya somos un árbol bien plantado”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Alma Campos

entries.title: Monday's Reds-Cubs game pushed up 30 minutes to 6:10 p.m. start
entries.summary: <p>The Cubs announced Monday morning that Monday night’s game against the Cincinnati Reds will start at 6:10 p.m., a half hour earlier than the scheduled 6:40 p.m. start, due to a forecast of storms later in the evening.</p><p>The Cubs have won five straight overall, and 11 in a row at Wrigley Field, after sweeping Arizona three straight over the weekend. They took over sole possession of first place in the National League Central on Saturday, and have now opened a two-game lead over the Reds and St. Louis Cardinals.</p><p>The Reds had begun the weekend in first place, the first time since 2006 they had entered May in first place, but were swept three straight by the Pirates in Pittsburgh prior to their arrival here.</p><p>The National Weather Service forecast this morning calls for thunderstorms to develop along and south of a cold front late this afternoon and evening. Strong to locally damaging winds will be the main hazard with the strongest storms, but a few storms may produce hail as well.<br></p>
entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Supreme Court restores access to abortion pill mifepristone through telehealth, mail and pharmacies
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON (AP) — <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/us-supreme-court" target="_blank" >The Supreme Court</a> on Monday restored broad access to the abortion pill mifepristone, blocking a ruling that had threatened to upend one of the main ways abortions are provided across the nation.</p><p>The order signed by Justice Samuel Alito temporarily allows women seeking abortions to obtain the pill at pharmacies or through the mail, without an in-person visit to a doctor.</p><p>Those rules had been in effect for several years until <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/abortion-pills-mail-louisiana-ruling-40d60a9bf6212480e527480757b603c3" target="_blank" >a federal appeals court imposed new restrictions</a> last week.</p><p>The majority of abortions in the U.S. are obtained through medications, usually a combination of mifepristone and a second drug, misoprostol. The availability of those drugs has blunted the impact of abortion bans that most Republican-led states have sought to enforce since a 2022 Supreme Court ruling that overturned Roe v. Wade.</p><p>Louisiana sued to restrict access to mifepristone, asserting that its availability undermined the ban there.</p><p>Some Democratic-led states have laws that seek to give legal protection to those who prescribe the drugs via telehealth to patients in states with bans.</p><p>Alito’s order will remain in effect for another week while both sides respond and the court more fully considers the issue.</p><p>Manufacturers of mifepristone filed emergency appeals asking the Supreme Court to step in.</p><p>Kristan Hawkins, president of the anti-abortion group Students for Life, decried Monday’s decision.</p><p>“Pill pushers receive every benefit of the doubt, including today, as Justice Alito allows pill traffickers and big pharma to operate temporarily while arguments are sent to the Court,” she said in a statement.</p><p>After Friday’s ruling from the appeals court, some groups that prescribe abortion pills by telehealth had planned to switch to misoprostol-only regimens.</p><p>Dr. Angel Foster, founder of The Massachusetts Abortion Access Project, said her organization was preparing to send misoprostol only on Monday afternoon but was able to switch back.</p><p>“Regardless of what happens with this regulatory issue, we and other groups will continue to provide high-quality abortion are to patients in all 50 states,” she said.</p>
entries.author: Geoff Mulvihill | AP

entries.title: Summer-like weather makes brief appearance in Chicago area
entries.summary: <p>Leave early, get outside and just enjoy the nearly 80 degree day that is in store for a limited time only, as temperatures cool into the 50s for the rest of the week, the National Weather Service says. </p><p>Monday will be the warmest day of the week by approximately 30 degrees. As of midday, it was 72 degrees at O'Hare Airport and may reach 80. </p><p>A cold front and some scattered storms Monday night will push out the warm air and bring back some chillier weather for the rest of the week.</p><p>“It's pretty common in springs to have these large fluctuations,” National Weather Service Meteorologist Ricky Castro said. “Eat outside for lunch. Leave early, if you can. Get outside. Enjoy it.”</p><p>Some warmer weather in the 60s is expected to roll around just in time for Mother’s Day weekend.<br></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Sky waive guard Hailey Van Lith
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> waived Hailey Van Lith, their No. 11 pick in the 2025 draft. Van Lith was a college standout but struggled to carve out a role in her rookie season, averaging 3.5 points per game in 12.4 minutes.</p><p>She spent the offseason rehabbing an ankle injury that hampered her first year. She scored 20 points in her first preseason game against the Mercury on a perfect 8-for-8 from the field, but most of her playing time came against Mercury reserves.</p><p>To get Van Lith, the Sky effectively gave up the No. 11 pick in the 2026 draft (Cotie McMahon) to the Lynx. The Lynx then traded that pick to the Mystics.</p><p>The Sky's roster now stands at 13 players. Second-year forward Maddy Westbeld, rookie Sydney Taylor, and rookie Aicha Coulibaly will likely be competing for the final spot. </p><p>The Sky will also have two development spots available. Van Lith could qualify for one of them, but first she will have to clear waivers, meaning that no other team claims her contract in the next 48 hours. ESPN reported that she's already in active discussions with other teams.</p><p>Final rosters are due on May 7.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: University of Chicago Press workers form union
entries.summary: <p>Employees at the University of Chicago Press have formed a union called UCP Workers Guild to push for fair wages, protections from artificial intelligence and better work conditions, according to an announcement Monday.</p><p>Out of more than 270 employees at the press, 139 workers are eligible to join the union, which is part of the Chicago News Guild. If all join, it would be the News Guild’s largest unit, according to the Guild.</p><p>"The university's financial crisis has tightened spending in several of the press's departments, slowed hiring and created a sense of uncertainty about the future for many UCP workers,” Adrienne Meyers, senior promotions manager at the press and UCP Workers Guild member, said in an emailed statement. “As of right now, the press has not experienced any layoffs due to the budget, and we hope our union will help protect and secure the stability of our workers."</p><p>UChicago didn’t respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Amid economic headwinds and threats to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/19/under-pressure-from-feds-university-of-chicago-cuts-ties-with-program-to-help-students-of-color-get-phds" target="_blank" >funding</a>, UChicago cut its budget deficit from $288 million in fiscal year 2024 to $160 million in fiscal year 2025, according to a university news release in December.</p><p>UCP Workers Guild has asked the university to decide whether to voluntarily recognize the union by Friday. If it declines, workers plan to hold an election with the National Labor Relations Board.</p><p>Workers eligible to join the union include the press’ three divisions: books, journals and the Chicago Distribution Center. Administrative staff in IT, finance and intellectual property can also join, according to Meyers.</p><p>Union-eligible positions include jobs in customer service, marketing, manuscript editing, book design, production and acquisitions. Excluded roles include those that have direct reports and members of the Chicago Distribution Center staff who are already organized with The International Brotherhood of Teamsters.</p><p>UCP Workers Guild’s mission statement calls for “protections amid the rise of generative AI, which threatens to degrade our job security, professional integrity and author relationships.”</p><p>The statement also asks to “demystify the press’s operational relationship to the university and the bureaucratic policies that prevent managers from hiring, retaining and rewarding staff.”</p><p>Griffin Reed, marketing associate at the press and UCP Workers Guild member, said in a news release, “My coworkers and I face low, stagnant wages; a lack of standardization across the press regarding our ability to work from home; and potential precarity in the form of layoffs, outsourcing or the introduction of AI into our workflow. I want a union to ensure that our jobs are protected and that we are paid fairly for our profit-generating labor.”</p><p>The union’s website said, “None of us should have to work second and third jobs to afford housing and groceries, while the university’s top administrators make multimillion-dollar salaries. The press is also a revenue-generating unit of the university, and that gives us leverage.”</p><p>The press was founded in 1890 as one of the three main divisions of the University of Chicago and remains part of it. It’s one of the country’s oldest and largest university presses and has published titles such as “The Chicago Manual of Style” and “A River Runs Through It” by Norman Maclean.</p><p>The press has published more than 11,000 books since its founding and currently has more than 5,000 books in print. It also publishes nearly 100 scholarly journals.</p><p>Many Chicago arts and cultural institutions, including the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2023/8/15/23833287/workers-art-institute-chicago-school-ratify-union" target="_blank" >Art Institute of Chicago</a>, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/work/2024/04/05/union-wave-afscme-newberry-library-chicago-museums-cultural-institutions" target="_blank" >Newberry Library</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/work/2025/03/06/shedd-aquarium-workers-union-penguin-trainer-fired-activism" target="_blank" >Shedd Aquarium</a>, have also joined a wave of labor organizing across the U.S. in recent years, especially since the COVID-19 pandemic.</p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Comisión que investiga el Operativo Midway Blitz ‘sienta las bases para una verdadera rendición de cuentas’
entries.summary: <p>Los agentes federales de inmigración actuaron de manera ilegal y con impunidad para llevar a cabo la agresiva campaña de deportación del presidente Donald Trump en Chicago, y lo hicieron con el firme apoyo del presidente y su administración, reveló un panel de responsabilidad estatal.</p><p>Después de pasar seis meses recopilando testimonios, grabaciones de video y registros legales, la Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/2026-04-iac-final-report.html" target="_blank" >publicó su informe final</a> el jueves, examinando el Operativo Midway Blitz y el presunto abuso llevado a cabo por los agentes federales en y alrededor de la ciudad el otoño pasado.</p><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker formó la comisión en octubre para investigar la conducta de los agentes federales durante el Operativo Midway Blitz. En una conferencia de prensa el jueves, Pritzker dijo que reunió al panel de expertos legales para llevar a cabo una investigación exhaustiva cuando se dio cuenta de que la administración de Trump no iba a responsabilizar a los agentes.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-020000" name="module-020000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"Estas audiencias e investigaciones demostraron que el Operativo Midway Blitz no fue una desafortunada colección de errores de juicio o abusos de poder limitados por actores individuales malintencionados", dijo Pritzker. "Fue un esfuerzo cuidadosamente orquestado y coordinado que comenzó con Donald Trump y fue ejecutado por Stephen Miller, Kristi Noem, Tom Homan, Greg Bovino y otros".</p><p>A principios de esta semana, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/28/panel-de-responsabilidad-de-pritzker-escucha-testimonios-sobre-las-acciones-del-operativo-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >la comisión realizó audiencias</a> y convocó a testigos y víctimas para hablar sobre varios de los principales puntos de conflicto durante el Operativo Midway Blitz. Esto incluyó los enfrentamientos entre residentes y agentes federales en <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/12/01/videos-de-camaras-corporales-muestran-las-tacticas-agresivas-de-los-federales-en-chicago-a-detalle" target="_blank" >La Villita</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/24/federales-realizan-arrestos-noroeste-lanzan-lacrimgeno-calle-residencial" target="_blank" >Lake View</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/28/residentes-del-noroeste-enfrentan-a-agentes-que-disparan-gas-lacrimogeno-cancelan-desfile-de-halloween" target="_blank" >Old Irving Park</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/15/tras-persecucion-en-calle-residencial-de-chicago-agentes-federales-embisten-vehiculo-y-lanzan-gas-lacrimogeno" target="_blank" >East Side</a> y Evanston.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8763d0" name="image-8763d0"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9dea696/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23e4c5c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2acc4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="El gobernador JB Pritzker habla en un podio." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2acc4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c943ce7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2acc4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb5%2Fe3%2F6996284547e2b64fdccdd2c58e3c%2Fimg-9407.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El gobernador JB Pritzker y su Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois discuten el informe final del panel que se publicó el jueves.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kaitlin Washburn/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El informe también nombra a agentes federales específicos que la comisión alega que violaron la ley. Uno de esos agentes es Charles Exum, el agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza que disparó cinco veces a Marimar Martínez después de que un automóvil con tres agentes de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) chocara con su vehículo el 4 de octubre en Brighton Park. Martínez y su abogado Christopher Parente <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/29/panel-de-responsabilidad-pide-cargos-criminales-por-mala-conducta-de-agentes-durante-operativo-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >testificaron ante la comisión el martes</a> sobre el tiroteo y sus secuelas.</p><p>Otros agentes vistos en varios videos no son nombrados porque la comisión carecía del poder para investigar a todos los agentes involucrados, dijo el juez Rubén Castillo, presidente de la comisión.</p><p>Por eso, el informe <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/ilac/documents/final-report/IAC-Referral-Letter-04-2026.pdf" target="_blank" >incluye una carta</a> refiriendo cargos a la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, Eileen O’Neill Burke, al Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD), a la fiscal del estado del Condado de Kane y a los departamentos de policía de Elgin, Franklin Park y Evanston.</p><p>"Está muy claro para mí", dijo Castillo en la conferencia de prensa del jueves, "que el Departamento de Justicia no va a investigar a estos agentes que cometieron estas atrocidades. Entonces, ¿qué queda? Lo que queda son nuestros fiscales locales, y en particular, nuestra fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, y se le referirá este informe. Ha dicho que no ha recibido nada. Bueno, hoy se le va a referir algo, y debería ser investigado".</p><p>Y si Burke "no quiere investigar", agregó Castillo, debería "dar un paso al lado y dejar que un fiscal especial entre y haga lo que necesita hacerse".</p><p>Castillo, un exjuez jefe del distrito federal, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/coalicion-dice-que-fiscal-del-condado-de-cook-eileen-oneill-burke-ignoro-la-violencia-de-ice" target="_blank" >es parte de una coalición que pide</a> un fiscal especial para investigar a los agentes federales.</p><p>La comisión carecía tanto de poder de citación como de poder judicial. Ahora depende de las fuerzas del orden locales continuar con la investigación de la comisión, dijo Pritzker.</p><p>"La comisión ha hecho mucho de lo que está en su poder hacer, para sentar las bases para una verdadera rendición de cuentas", dijo. "Ha recopilado la evidencia. Ha escuchado a víctimas inocentes. Ha identificado a los culpables. Pero tomará la valentía y convicción de todos nosotros, en todos los niveles de gobierno, para seguir avanzando y asegurarnos de que estas conclusiones se acompañen de acción",</p><p>En respuesta, Burke emitió un comunicado el jueves manteniendo que no puede presentar cargos hasta que haya recibido una investigación completa de una agencia de aplicación de la ley.</p><p>"Elogiamos la valentía de cada testigo que testificó sobre sus angustiosas experiencias ante la Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois", dijo Burke en el comunicado. "No hay duda de que el Operativo Midway Blitz ha traumatizado y dañado nuestras comunidades. Esperamos recibir el informe completo y trabajaremos con nuestras fuerzas del orden locales, estatales y del condado para revisar el material".</p><p>El informe final incluye <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/ilac/documents/final-report/IAC-FinalReport-AppendixB-Policy-Reccommendations.pdf" target="_blank" >varias otras recomendaciones</a> para las autoridades locales, estatales y federales, tales como:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-f9cd70f0-47fb-11f1-b74a-b3c832391785"><li>El Departamento de Seguridad Nacional "debe mantener bajo control el uso de agentes químicos, fuerza física y persecuciones vehiculares por parte de los agentes". El departamento también debe asegurarse de que todos los agentes usen cámaras corporales y prohibir que los agentes se cubran la cara y oculten su identidad mientras están en el trabajo.</li><li>El Congreso "debe enmendar la Ley de Inmigración y Nacionalidad para poner fin a los arrestos sin orden judicial por cuestiones de ejecución civil y prohibir las detenciones discriminatorias que se basen en la raza, etnia, lugar de trabajo, acento o idioma".</li><li>El estado de Illinois debe trabajar con los gobiernos locales y organizaciones sin fines de lucro para "aliviar el daño económico causado por el Operativo Midway Blitz, expandir el acceso a servicios legales y apoyo en planificación para la preparación familiar, y prevenir más interrupciones en la vida cívica por actividades de aplicación de la ley de inmigración".</li></ul><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Estudiantes y maestros de CPS marchan en solidaridad con los trabajadores
entries.summary: <p>Ricardo Juárez, de once años, se acercó a las personas que atendían cada mesa en el parque frente a su escuela y preguntó con voz segura: “¿Qué hacen ustedes por la comunidad?”.</p><p>“Sólo quiero escuchar y ver de qué manera pueden ayudarnos”, dijo Ricardo, quien es estudiante de la Orozco Academy, ubicada en el vecindario de Pilsen en Chicago.</p><p>Las mesas, que representaban a organizaciones como la Cruz Roja Americana, el Pilsen Wellness Center, Family Focus y el Pilsen Neighbors Community Council, se instalaron como parte de una jornada cívica de acción que tuvo lugar este viernes en escuelas, parques y otros puntos de la ciudad.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure comunitaria de educación y recursos el viernes. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4db4b9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6854x3847+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F02%2F8af017244216ba654ebc138a5f4c%2Fmaydaycivics-260502-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c2e3ea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6854x3847+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F02%2F8af017244216ba654ebc138a5f4c%2Fmaydaycivics-260502-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4db4b9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6854x3847+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F02%2F8af017244216ba654ebc138a5f4c%2Fmaydaycivics-260502-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ricardo Juárez, de once años (centro), y otros estudiantes de quinto grado de la Orozco Academy conversan con representantes del Instituto Health Sciences Career Academy durante una feria comunitaria de educación y recursos el viernes. La actividad formó parte de un esfuerzo más amplio para conmemorar el 1 de mayo, o Día Internacional de los Trabajadores, fecha que rinde homenaje a las luchas y victorias del movimiento obrero.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) llegaron a un acuerdo con el Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU) para designar el 1 de mayo —también conocido como el Día Internacional de los Trabajadores— como un día de participación cívica en todo el distrito escolar. Algunos estudiantes realizaron paseos durante el horario escolar, mientras que otros recibieron lecciones de educación cívica en la escuela.</p><p>El CTU había solicitado que el distrito suspendiera las clases; sin embargo, las escuelas permanecieron abiertas con normalidad y CPS accedió a proporcionar autobuses y almuerzos. Algunos de esos autobuses trasladaron a los estudiantes a una manifestación de Operation PUSH, en el lado sur de la ciudad, así como a otros eventos comunitarios; otros transportaron a los alumnos a una concentración vespertina en Union Park, desde donde marcharon hacia Daley Plaza.</p><p>Algunos miembros de CTU expresaron su decepción por el hecho de que CPS dificultara la participación en los paseos. Las escuelas tuvieron que completar planes de seguridad de 11 páginas, incluso si los estudiantes se dirigían a tan solo unas pocas cuadras de distancia.</p><p>Los funcionarios de CPS afirmaron haber trabajado hasta altas horas de la madrugada el 1 de mayo para autorizar las excursiones. Finalmente, se aprobaron 40 paseos, lo que permitió que 2,200 estudiantes participaran en actividades fuera del recinto escolar.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-960000" name="image-960000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Estudiantes participan en una actividad durante una concentración del Primero de mayo y en un paseo educativo en la sede de Operation PUSH en Kenwood el viernes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Aunque las cifras aún se están consolidando, CPS informó que la asistencia en las escuelas primarias fue normal el viernes, mientras que, al parecer, un número ligeramente más alto al habitual de estudiantes de secundaria tomó el día libre. La ley de Illinois permite a los estudiantes de escuelas intermedias y secundarias una ausencia justificada por motivos cívicos.</p><p>Aproximadamente el 13% de los maestros de CPS estuvieron ausentes el viernes, una cifra superior a la de los días anteriores de la semana, según datos del distrito. No obstante, los funcionarios del distrito escolar aseguraron haber “mantenido con éxito la cobertura instruccional” mediante la contratación de 2,600 maestros sustitutos y el envío de personal de la oficina central a 76 escuelas.</p><p>Andrew Martinek, maestro de la Hancock College Prep, quien llevó a una clase de estudiantes de primer año de secundaria a la organización Operation PUSH, expresó su entusiasmo por haber tenido la oportunidad de que sus alumnos escucharan a funcionarios públicos y activistas.</p><p>“Es una oportunidad para que nuestros estudiantes tomen conciencia de su propio poder”, afirmó Martinek, quien sostiene que sus alumnos de noveno grado apenas están comenzando a descubrir su propia voz. “Todo aquello que contribuya a empoderarlos e infundirles la confianza necesaria para que puedan alzar su voz ante el poder mientras crecen, eso es precisamente de lo que se trata todo esto”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> sobre la comunidad y lo que significa protestar</b></h3><p>En Orozco, unas docenas de estudiantes salieron por la entrada principal el viernes por la mañana y se dirigieron al cercano Harrison Park para aprender cómo operan las organizaciones y negocios locales de la comunidad.</p><p>Las organizaciones repartieron dulces y pequeños juguetes, pero Ricardo comentó que no le interesaban mucho esas golosinas.</p><p>“Estoy aprendiendo cómo las empresas de atención médica, como Sinai o la Cruz Roja, ayudan a las personas necesitadas”, dijo Ricardo. “Estoy aprendiendo cosas que no sabía antes”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7a0000" name="image-7a0000"></a> <picture Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Esto incluye estudiar la historia del Primero de mayo y cómo el movimiento obrero propició mejores condiciones laborales y salarios más altos.</p><p>A eso del mediodía, los estudiantes se congregaron en el patio de la escuela Orozco para participar en una manifestación. Se sentaron sobre el campo de pasto artificial, sosteniendo en alto las pancartas de protesta que habían confeccionado en clase a primera hora del día.</p><p>Pequeñas voces coreaban “¡No más ICE!”, en referencia a la agencia que ha ayudado a llevar a cabo la campaña de deportaciones masivas del presidente Donald Trump.</p><p>Para algunos, como Ricardo, era su primera protesta.</p><div de intensa actividad de control migratorio durante el otoño. El personal comentó que la escuela <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/13/como-fue-que-los-agentes-de-inmigracion-alteraron-las-escuelas-de-cps-este-otono" target="_blank" ><u>tuvo que mantener a los estudiantes en el interior de las instalaciones en varias ocasiones durante ese periodo</u></a>, por lo que el tema estaba muy presente en la mente de todos. Los estudiantes que intervinieron en la manifestación se centraron en el hecho de provenir de familias de inmigrantes que han trabajado arduamente para brindarles una vida mejor.</p><p>“Este es un gran día para recordarles a todos que, sin los inmigrantes, este país no sería lo que es hoy”, afirmó un alumno de quinto grado.</p><h3><b>Un enfoque en la mejora de los vecindarios y en honrar a los trabajadores</b></h3><p>Esa misma mañana en Kenwood, cerca de 500 estudiantes, desde niños pequeños de kinder hasta alumnos de secundaria, entraron en fila al antiguo auditorio de Operation PUSH. Los entusiastas miembros del CTU los recibieron con sus camisetas rojas.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a sede de Operation PUSH, en el vecindario de Kenwood.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los oradores hablaron sobre los derechos de los trabajadores y el clima político actual, pero su mensaje se centró más en la necesidad de involucrarse y en la idea de que los jóvenes deben ser escuchados.</p><p>La presidenta de CTU, Stacy Davis Gates, les dijo a los estudiantes que escucharían a los oradores hablar sobre grandes ideas, pero que ella quería que se enfocaran en aquello que desean ver mejorar en sus propios vecindarios y escuelas.</p><p>Los instó a decirles a los líderes “lo que se merecen”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 2x" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Stacey Davis Gates, presidenta del Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago, habla sobre el Primero de mayo durante una manifestación y una visita educativa en la sede de Operation PUSH, ubicada en el lado sur de la ciudad.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Estudiantes de la secundaria Hancock, ubicada en el suroeste de la ciudad, estuvieron entre los primeros en llegar. El día adquirió un significado especial para estos estudiantes, la mayoría de los cuales son latinos. Como ocurre en muchas escuelas, los alumnos de Hancock participaron en una salida masiva de los salones a principios de este año para protestar contra las políticas migratorias de la administración de Trump.</p><p>Joel Gutiérrez, estudiante de primer año de secundaria, comentó que decidió asistir al paseo porque representaba una oportunidad de visitar un lugar nuevo. Pero también señaló que el Primero de mayo era importante para él como una forma de honrar a los trabajadores de mano de obra, como su padre.</p><p>“Él trabaja en una fábrica y sé que se esfuerza mucho por mí”, dijo Gutiérrez. “Eso lo valoro mucho”.</p><p>Christian Martínez, quien también es estudiante de primer año en Hancock, mostró su apoyo a los maestros de CPS que deseaban tomarse el día para honrar la labor de los sindicatos. Afirmó que los maestros trabajan muy duro y que la mayoría de ellos son buenos profesionales.</p><p>“Hablé con mi mamá al respecto”, comentó. “Estuvo totalmente de acuerdo”.</p><p></p><h3><b>Algunos estudiantes faltan a clases para hacer escuchar sus voces</b></h3><p>El sonido de tambores, discursos, cánticos y conversaciones resonó en todo Union Park mientras miles de personas se congregaban para la manifestación anual del Primero de mayo.</p><p>Mientras muchos estudiantes viajaron al parque del Near West Side junto a compañeros de clase y maestros, otros como Isabella López, de 17 años, faltaron a clases el viernes como parte de un boicot nacional bajo el lema “no trabajo, no escuela, no compras”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Isabella López (a la izquierda) faltó a clases el viernes para asistir a la manifestación y marcha anual del Primero de mayo. Quería asistir para alzar su voz en contra de las agresivas políticas de control migratorio implementadas por la administración de Trump.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Emmanuel Camarillo/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Esta estudiante de tercer año de secundaria en la Rickover Naval Academy llevaba las banderas de México y Puerto Rico cubriendo sus hombros, a fin de representar a ambos lados de su familia. Isabella afirmó que asistió a la protesta para alzar la voz en contra de las políticas migratorias severas de la administración de Trump.</p><p>Dijo que no le preocupaba que su ausencia escolar jugara en su contra, ya que se trataba de una buena causa y las personas cercanas a ella lo sabían.</p><p>“Era importante para mí venir aquí hoy para ser la voz de aquellas personas que se sienten de séptimo grado de la Escuela Primaria Funston en Logan Square, se dirigió a la protesta junto a varias docenas de compañeros de clase tras asistir a la escuela, según relató su maestro. Janiyah comentó que pasaron el día elaborando letreros y preparándose para la manifestación.</p><p>Su letrero decía: “El poder del pueblo es más fuerte que las personas en el poder”.</p><p>Los estudiantes señalaron que los jóvenes que asistieron a las protestas están aprendiendo que sus voces importan y que pueden influir en lo que sucede en el país. Se mostraron en desacuerdo con la idea de que deberían estar en clase en lugar de estar marchando en las calles.</p><p>“Los estudiantes también tenemos algo que decir en estos asuntos, pues somos los futuros adultos, la generación que viene”, afirmó Isabella.</p><p>Y si los jóvenes desean marcar la diferencia y generar cambios positivos, añadió, “así es como empezamos”.<br></p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

entries.title: Yankees broadcaster John Sterling, known for unique home run calls, dies at 87
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — John Sterling, the longtime radio broadcaster known for extravagant, individualized home run calls and declaring "theee Yankees win!" after each of New York's victories, died Monday. He was 87.</p><p>Sterling had a heart attack and bypass surgery this winter and had returned to his home in Edgewater, New Jersey, where he was cared for by health aides. He died Monday at Englewood Hospital, according to his former wife, Jennifer.</p><p>"John Sterling breathed life and excitement into Yankees games for 36 years while wearing his passion for baseball and the Yankees on his sleeve," the team said in a statement. "He informed and entertained generations of fans with a theatrical and unapologetic style that was uniquely his own. John treasured his role as the voice of the New York Yankees, and his enthusiasm for the art of broadcasting perfectly complemented our city and our fans. The symmetry between John and his audience was both undeniable and magical, and his signature calls will resonate for as long as we put on pinstripes — especially after every Yankees win."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Remembering the legendary John Sterling and some of his most famous calls ❤️🎙️ <a href="https://t.co/cKlpvEgnFI">pic.twitter.com/cKlpvEgnFI</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2051328068673782030?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">May 4, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>He had called 5,631 games — 5,420 regular season plus 211 postseason — when he retired in April 2024 just after the season's start, citing fatigue. Sterling broadcast 5,060 consecutive games from September 1989 through July 2019 after beginning with the Yankees as a pregame host. He came out of retirement to broadcast Yankees games during the 2024 postseason.</p><p>Sterling's call for a player's home run became as treasured a part of a Yankees identity as an initial set of pinstripes or a championship ring. As rookies prepared for debuts and former opponents arrived in trades, fans speculated how he would label the newcomer's first longball.</p><p>From "Bernie goes boom! Bern, baby, Bern!" for Bernie Williams, to "It's a Jeter jolt!" for Derek to "It's an A-bomb from A-Rod!" for Alex Rodriguez, "The Giambino!" for Jason Giambi and "A thrilla from Godzilla!" for Hideki Matsui, Sterling created personal stamps resonating from the clubhouse to the bleachers.</p><p>"It wasn't meant that way. I just happened to do something for Bernie Williams. He hit a home run and I said, `Bern, baby, Bern!' And it kind of mushroomed from there," Sterling said at the time of his retirement. "But it never was intended for every player, because, frankly, I'm not smart enough to do something for every player. But I did the best I could, and it's amazing what started out as — became so big."</p><p>"I did say 'A-bomb from A-Rod!' when he hit a home run and I did say: 'Robbie Canó, don't you know,' and I think those were pretty good," Sterling said of calls for Rodriguez and Robinson Canó.</p><p>Born Josh Sloss on July 4, 1938, Sterling grew up in Manhattan and left college to work for radio stations. He had wanted to be a broadcaster since hearing "The Eddie Bracken Show" in the 1940s.</p><p>"I didn't want to be Eddie Bracken. I wanted to be the guy who says: 'Live from Hollywood!'" Sterling said. "And I knew that maybe a year or two later, but before puberty I knew I was going to be on the air. And it really helped me because I didn't worry about school, because I knew what I was going to do. And it was a good thing because I was a terrible student — terrible."</p><p>He started his radio career in 1960 at a station in Wellsville, New York.</p><p>"I was preparing this all my life. It was easy," he said. "I could always open my mouth and talk."</p><p>Sterling cited Mel Allen, Russ Hodges and Jim Karvellas as influences. He wound up joining Allen in the history of memorable Yankees broadcasters along with Red Barber, Phil Rizzuto, Bill White and Frank Messer.</p><p>Sterling announced the NBA's Washington Bullets and Morgan State football in his early years and gained fame for shrieking "Islanders goal! Islanders goal!" during the hockey team's games from 1975-78. He broadcast for the NBA's Nets from 1975-80.</p><p>Sterling's first connection with the Yankees was during WMCA pregame radio talk shows from 1971-78. He moved to Atlanta and worked for the Braves from 1982-87 and Hawks from 1981-89 before switching to the Yankees, where he replaced Hank Greenwald.</p><p>Sterling was seldom in the clubhouse and dressed in Brooks Brothers suits even though he was on the radio.</p><p>He partnered with Jay Johnstone (1989-90), Joe Angel (1991), Michael Kay (1992-2001), Charley Steiner (2002-04) and Suzyn Waldman (since 2005). Sterling and Waldman were inducted into the New York State Broadcasters Hall of Fame in 2016.</p><p>He was married to the former Jennifer Contreras from 1993 to 2004. In addition to her, he is survived by triplets Bradford, Derek and Veronica, and daughter Abigail.</p><p>Sterling was proud of his unique style.</p><p>"Harry Caray told me some years ago," he recalled in 2024 of the famous Cubs and White Sox broadcaster, "and he says, 'John, all the guys are great. We just have different styles.' And no one has a more different style than I have."<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Former Ald. George Cardenas forms exploratory committee to run for mayor, potentially dividing Latino vote
entries.summary: <p>A second Latino candidate — former Ald. George Cardenas <sup></sup> — is laying the groundwork to run for mayor of Chicago in a move that could have a signficant impact on a crowded field.</p><p>Cardenas said Monday he’s forming an exploratory committee to run for mayor, even as he<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/17/cook-county-review-board-primary-results" > seeks re-election</a> in November as a commissioner of the Cook County Board of Review.</p><p>Although he barely gathered enough signatures to survive a petition challenge for the office he now holds, Cardenas believes he has the unique mix of experience necessary to tackle Chicago’s vexing problems.</p><p>“I have business experience. I’m a management consultant. I’m an expert in procurement. I was in the Council for 20 years. I know the bodies. I know what worked… with Mayor [Rahm] Emanuel, what didn’t. I was floor leader for [then-Mayor Lori] Lightfoot. I’ve been there. I have the experience,” Cardenas, 61, told the Sun-Times.</p><p>Unlike Mayor Brandon Johnson, who struggled to pass his last two budgets and had to swallow the City Council’s choice of a new Zoning Committee chair, Cardenas said, “I have relationships. I’m a builder of relationships. That’s what I’ve been throughout my career.”</p><p>Cardenas’ entry into the already crowded 2027 race for mayor would divide a Latino vote that has comprised just 20% of the electorate in recent Chicago elections.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/04/16/brandon-johnson-alexi-giannoulias-susana-mendoza-potential-mayoral-bids-campaign-fundraising" >Johnson has just $813,125 in campaign cash months before mayoral race; Giannoulias has $18.3 million</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Latino voters determined to support one of their own would be forced to choose between Cardenas and his longtime political rival, retiring State Comptroller Susana Mendoza.</p><p>“I’m not running as a Hispanic. I’m running as a Chicagoan. I’m not running just because I can get the Hispanic vote. When I ran for the Board of Review, I got a sizeable part of the vote in the white community… and the white ethnic bloc,” Cardenas said. "Yes, I’m Mexican heritage. But I want to talk about numbers. I want to talk about what’s going on with the [spending] and the management of the city. That’s what I’m about.”</p><p>Veteran political operative Victor Reyes ran the Hispanic Democratic Organization at the center of the city hiring scandal under former Mayor Richard M. Daley. It was in that role that he helped Cardenas upset incumbent Ald. Ray Frias (12th) in 2003. Mendoza supported Frias against Cardenas in that race and has been a Cardenas adversary every since.</p><p>Reyes, who does not have a horse in the 2027 mayoral race, views Cardenas' entry as a significant development because he’s won races for the Cook County Board of Review in a “humongous” district that has a “large white and suburban population.”</p><p>“He’s shown that he’s able to get votes from communities that he would need in order to be a viable candidate” for mayor, Reyes said.</p><p>“If this number stays in, you could get into the runoff with as low as 16 to 17 percent… You divide up the pie, then you need a smaller piece of the pie. It also means that Johnson needs a smaller piece of the pie.”</p><p>If Cardenas and Mendoza both join the mayoral race and stay in, the biggest beneficiary could be Johnson, Reyes said. The biggest loser could be Illinois Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias, the frontrunner with $18.3 million in his campaign war chest, a heavy chunk of it from building trade unions.</p><p>“This hurts Giannoulias as much as it hurts Mendoza,” Reyes said of Cardenas.</p><p>“It’s gonna split the Latino vote, so it hurts Mendoza. But it also hurts Giannoulias because George has crossover viability. He’s more of a moderate. So is Susana. But they’re gonna eat into each other’s bases… Both will not get into a runoff. One of those two has the potential, and they both [can] create a problem for Giannoulias.”</p><p>Cardenas' 20-year record in the Council is likely to be both an asset and a liability.</p><p>"If he makes it to the party and can work with the City Council, that's a positive. But he has votes that he will have to defend" including a virtual avalanche of tax increases, as well as the widely despised parking meter deal, Reyes said.</p><p>Cardenas said Monday he is proud of his record and is prepared to defend it. He's not intimidated by Giannoulias' massive campaign war chest.</p><p>"I just watched the Kentucky Derby... Golden Tempo came out of nowhere. He was dead last," Cardenas said.</p><p>"We're all like Golden Tempo. We're a little bit of that," Cardenas added. "He comes from dynastic blood lines. All of that stuff doesn't mean you win. Heart and passion has something to do with it." </p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Chicago's Leo High School principal surprised with prestigious Golden Apple award
entries.summary: <p>Shaka Rawls, the principal at Leo Catholic High School in Auburn Gresham, knew something was up on Monday when he saw all the cars in the school’s parking lot.</p><p>What he didn’t know was that all those visitors, including friends, family and elected officials, were there to congratulate him for <a class="Link" href="https://www.goldenapple.org/news/shaka-rowls-2026-golden-apple-award-recipient" target="_blank" ><u>winning the Golden Apple Award for Excellence in Leadership</u></a>, one of the most prestigious honors for Illinois principals.</p><p>Visitors and students, many wearing orange Leo High School hoodies over their ties, filled the school’s auditorium for the surprise. They let out a joyous roar when Rawls walked through the doors. Blue pompoms shook in the air. A wide smile spread across Rawls’ face when he realized what was happening.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Students celebrate with a chant during a surprise announcement of the Golden Apple award to principal Shaka Rawls at Leo High School.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“The most important thing is to experience this with the students,” Rawls said. “The award is for leadership, but essentially the award is for the work that we’ve done. I’m sharing this award with my students and the faculty at Leo High School.”</p><p>Rawls, principal at Leo since 2016, was selected as one of the leadership award winners from among nearly 100 nominees. Golden Apple winners, which will also include teachers, are being surprised with awards this month. All winners get a cash award of $5,000, and leaders also get $5,000 for their school.</p><p>Under Rawls’ leadership, Leo seniors have a 100% college acceptance rate and the share of ninth graders who are on track to graduate within four years has increased by 20 percentage points, according to the Golden Apple Foundation, which awards educators the prestigious prizes each year.</p><p>But he isn’t being recognized only for academic achievements.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-140000" name="image-140000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b7c5271/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fe0%2F8dbfd7a141ee8fc56a58b833274b%2Fgoldenappleleo-260505-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28eccd7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fe0%2F8dbfd7a141ee8fc56a58b833274b%2Fgoldenappleleo-260505-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Principal Shaka Rawls used his Golden Apple award as a teaching moment for his students: He told him that he had applied for the prestigious honor in the past, but hadn’t won. He kept trying.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Rawls also spends time mentoring students. He sits with them at lunch and lets them know that he is available if they need someone to talk to. Rawls’ role as a leader at the school feels personal to him because he once walked its halls as a student. He graduated from Leo in 1993 and wants to give the students access to the kinds of resources and relationships he has now, but didn’t back then.</p><p>“When I look at every student in this building I see me,” Rawls said.</p><p>Rawls had to be convinced to step in as principal a decade ago. He was then an assistant principal at a Chicago charter school and was hesitant to make the leap because he knew the pressures of the job. But his love for the school won out.</p><p>“He understood that there was a need for the type of skill that he could bring,” said Dan McGrath, the former long-time president of the high school. McGrath credits Rawls for helping turn around the school, noting that around the time he was hired the community was concerned it’d be closed due to low enrollment.</p><p>“We don’t hear that much anymore,” McGrath said, adding that enrollment has doubled under Rawls’ tenure at a time when other <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/01/26/archdiocese-catholic-school-closing-jerome-bruno-richard-francis-borgia-stanislaus-kostka" target="_blank" ><u>local Catholic schools are losing students</u></a>. There are 250 students at Leo and they are predominantly Black.</p><p>McGrath most appreciates Rawls’ rapport with the students, and what he represents for them.</p><p>“He’s from the area, he’s a Leo graduate, they look at him and they see a young, dynamic, energetic Black man in this position of authority,” McGrath said. “I think it rings a bell that if he can do that, I might be able to do that.”</p><p>That relationship was apparent in the auditorium. As Rawls spoke to the crowd some students yelled out “you the man!” Rawls and the students belted out the school fight song together to close out the ceremony.</p><div class="Enhancement"
