class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Andre Rawls, principal Shaka Rawls’ mother, smiles as her son receives a leadership award Monday from the Golden Apple Foundation.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Rawls’ mother, Andre Rawls, said her son is continuing a legacy that made her enroll her boys at Leo in the first place.</p><p>“Leo had a reputation for graduating young men that were leaders, that would do well in the community,” Andre Rawls said. “We wanted that for our children, as well.”</p><p>The award came with a lesson in perseverance, too. When he addressed his students, Rawls told them he applied in 2022 and didn’t get it.</p><p>“It doesn’t matter if you fail,” Rawls said, “never ever give up.”</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Walgreens to close Chatham store, citing theft and violence
entries.summary: <p>Dozens of South Side residents gathered on Monday outside of a Chatham Walgreens, waving signs saying “Senior Lives Matter” and “End Corporate Abandonment,” after learning the store will be closing. </p><p>Daycare owner Latonya Mitchell said her center's families were alarmed that the pharmacy would be closing. Many of the children she cares for at Exceptional Little Leaders Academy are low-income and their families visit the Walgreens because it's within walking distance, she said. The children she serves need medication for conditions like ADHD and asthma.</p><p>“It's unacceptable to these children that they can't get the things that they need from a place like this, and then you gotta leave us destitute and in a desert,” Mitchell said. “That's not fair to us, and that's not fair to these children. That's not fair to our seniors, and that's not fair to the members of this community.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Latonya Mitchell speaking during a news conference outside of the Walgreens in Chatham that will close on June 4.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Walgreens said in a statement that theft and violent incidents led to their decision to close the store at 8628 S. Cottage Grove Ave. It's the only Walgreens in the neighborhood, with the nearest location 1.3 miles away at 1616 E. 87th St.</p><p>“Despite a range of efforts, including previous operating adjustments, these ongoing safety challenges have made it increasingly difficult to maintain a secure environment for our team members and customers,” the company said. “While this was not an easy decision, safety must remain our top priority.”</p><p>Employees at the Chatham store will be able to transfer to other locations, the company said.</p><p>The store is slated to close on June 4, the Deerfield-based company said. Following the closure, prescriptions will “be automatically transferred” to the nearest stores to “ensure uninterrupted service.” Pharmacy customers will also be able to receive free prescription delivery for 90 days.</p><p>During a news conference outside the store, residents and local leaders said the pharmacy's closure would negatively impact the community. </p><p>“Walgreens has decided that they don't want to tolerate us no more,” Ald. William Hall (6th) said. “They had no dignity when they closed the door, but we showed dignity with our money to invest to open the store. Let's not forget that it's TIF money that got this here. And so now that the TIF is dry, Walgreens, all of a sudden is drying up. And so again, what Walgreens has showed us is that they're not even willing to sit down at the table to have a conversation with the community.”</p><p>A portion of the area, which includes Walgreens, was designated as a <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/dcd/supp_info/tif/87th_cottage_grovetif.html" target="_blank" >tax increment financing district</a> in 2002 with its expiration occurring this year.</p><p>The area has a large population of older adults, many of whom don't drive and rely on the pharmacy for medication access, Hall said.</p><p>“Our seniors deserve better than silence and sudden change,” Hall said.</p><p>Since 2025, at least seven Walgreens locations on the South Side have shuttered or will close, including the area’s only specialty pharmacy at 2351 E. 71st St., which will close May 19. </p><p>Walgreens had announced plans in 2025 to close five locations on the South and West sides, as part of a plan to shutter <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/01/16/walgreens-closing-stores-south-west-sides" >1,200 locations by 2027</a>. Walgreens, which was acquired by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/03/06/walgreens-acquired-sycamore-partners-private-equity-10-billion" >Sycamore Partners</a> in 2025, has previously said it would prioritize closing poor-performing stores owned by the company, or locations where the leases are expiring.</p><p>“While we won’t be able to keep every store open, closures are always a last resort,” Walgreens said Monday. “We expect to close fewer than 100 stores in 2026 and have approved four new store openings.”</p><p>The South and West sides have fewer pharmacy locations than other areas of the city. Access to the two largest pharmacy chains in Chicago — Walgreens and CVS — is much higher in the city’s white communities than in Black or Latino areas, a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/8/8/23288677/pharmacy-access-south-west-sides-contraception-black-brown-residents" >2022 Sun-Times analysis</a> showed. Of Walgreen's more than 100 Chicago locations, about one-third are on the South and West sides.</p><p>“Walgreens has not said what they're going to do to fill the gap in this community,” Hall said. “When Walgreens opened years ago, everybody thought Walgreens was the best option. Now that Walgreens is leaving, we have no option. But years ago, we had Black pharmacies all up and down King Drive … but Walgreens came and swiped ... them out.”</p><p>Hall wants Walgreens to redesign the Chatham location and give the community a five year commitment to stay in the neighborhood.</p><p>Resident Daryl Smith was clutching a bag stuffed to the brim with medications.</p><p>“I'm holding a bag with medicine that my mother takes every single day for the rest of her life, every pill bottle in here, by the way, has a Walgreens cap on it,” Smith said. “I live in the 16th ward. They closed the 63rd and King Drive [location]. They closed 63rd and Halsted. … I shouldn’t have to drive across town to find a Walgreens on my lunch break.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2025/01/16/walgreens-closing-stores-south-west-sides" >Walgreens closing 5 stores on South and West sides</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/8/8/23288677/pharmacy-access-south-west-sides-contraception-black-brown-residents" >You’ve heard of food deserts. Pharmacy deserts are just as bad.</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Mariah Rush

entries.title: CeaseFire founder says anti-violence strategy he launched in Chicago can fight 'disease' of authoritarianism
entries.summary: <p>Three decades ago, Dr. Gary Slutkin took on Chicago’s violence problem with a solution he called CeaseFire, which he said would inoculate the city from the “disease” of shootings as he did with public health emergencies like AIDS.</p><p>Now he’s applying the same thinking to an even broader problem: government authoritarianism.</p><p>Slutkin was scheduled to speak about his theory Monday night at the University of Chicago’s Harris School of Public Policy.</p><p>In an interview beforehand, the epidemiologist turned international violence prevention expert says his concept of authoritarian violence as a disease is a natural extension of his earlier CeaseFire work, which he and others credit with decreases in violence in Chicago and elsewhere. </p><p>Many other anti-violence groups in Chicago have been based on the CeaseFire model of having "interrupters" negotiating truces on the street.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/public-safety/2025/06/06/violence-intervention-frederick-seaton-street-outreach-institute-nonviolence-chicago-cure-violence" target="_blank" >Why is Chicago violence plummeting? Some credit street outreach workers.</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>According to Slutkin, crackdowns, political intimidation and dehumanizing rhetoric spread in waves, just like the epidemics of gun violence, AIDS and tuberculosis.</p><p>Slutkin presents his theory in his new book, "<a class="Link" href="https://tracking.us.nylas.com/l/ce7fe059b99a4b09ae2d994e444b9deb/2/3c0817a40f095504d1a33b056e312d12d34991990e8c3c955a6b30be20d6de8a?cache_buster=1777555150" target="_blank" ><u>The end of violence: Eliminating the World’s Most Dangerous Epidemic</u></a>."</p><p>He says authoritarian leaders are like “superspreaders” who use their power and platforms to “infect” their populations on a mass scale. State violence and imprisonment are symptoms of those diseased regimes, he says.</p><p>He says his book isn’t political in the sense that he’s not focusing on specific world leaders. He says he mentions President Donald Trump only once — in a section of the book about the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol.</p><p>Still, many examples of the authoritarian violence Slutkin highlights have occurred during Trump’s most recent administration.</p><p>They include: Department of Homeland Security agents shooting and roughing up protesters and immigrants; U.S. military attacks on Venezuela, Iran and other countries; U.S. threats of taking over Greenland; and even cutbacks in U.S. food and health programs that he says will lead to a surge in deaths here and abroad.</p><p>“This was done in a very cruel way,” he says.</p><p>But he insists his focus isn’t on any one political figure.</p><p>“This isn’t about a person,” he says. “It’s about the disorder.”</p><p>Authoritarianism, he adds, is rising worldwide — not just in the United States, and in some places far more deeply entrenched.</p><p>In Chicago, long before formal “violence interrupter” programs took hold, communities relied on public education campaigns, neighborhood mobilization and social pressure to push back against violence.</p><p>During recent DHS immigration enforcement efforts, similar community responses surfaced in Chicago, Minnesota and other cities, where public backlash helped shape broader political reactions, including the Trump administration pulling back on its most aggressive immigration strategies, he says.</p><p>Change, he suggests, often starts at the ground level — with ordinary people questioning behavior, rejecting dehumanization and holding others accountable.</p><p>“It filters upward,” he says, describing how community pressure can influence institutions and even elected officials.</p><p>But sustaining that energy is difficult.</p><p>He points to “epidemic fatigue” — exhaustion that sets in when crises pile up, from economic uncertainty to immigration enforcement to the threat of war. The confusion, he says, can paralyze people.</p><p>The solution, in his view, is disciplined concentration on the problem: “Focus on the violence." That means educating the public, calling out harmful actions and challenging those who encourage or tolerate them. It also means rethinking protests not as attacks on individuals but as broader rejections of violent behavior itself.</p><p>Even those at the center of violence, he argues, often want to change. From gang leaders in U.S. cities to cartel figures in Latin America, many are trapped in what he calls a “stuck” system. Without alternatives, he says they remain locked in cycles of harm.</p><p>Authoritarian leaders may face a similar bind. With grim odds — exile, imprisonment or assassination — many act out of fear, he says, clinging to power as a means of survival.</p><p>“They need a way out,” Slutkin says — one that offers both safety and a path to save face.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-cb0000" name="image-cb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" entries.author: Frank Main

entries.title: Illinois touts high vaccination rates among school kids despite federal efforts to limit them
entries.summary: <p>Despite a federal effort to decrease the number of vaccines required for children, new data shows vaccination rates remain high among school-aged children in Illinois.</p><p>The measles vaccination rate is 96.8% across the state, according to the newly released data from the Illinois Department of Public Health. The dashboard uses data from the Illinois State Board of Education that is collected from school districts across the state.</p><p>Other parts of the country have experienced measles outbreaks recently, including in South Carolina where nearly 1,000 people contracted the highly contagious disease since October. For months, Illinois officials have pushed back on efforts led by Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services, to limit childhood vaccinations.</p><p>Earlier this year, the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/05/us-drops-the-number-of-vaccines-it-recommends-for-every-child" target="_blank" ><u>reduced the number of recommended vaccines</u></a>, but Illinois health officials <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/02/05/illinois-endorses-american-academy-of-pediatrics-vaccine-schedule-despite-changes-to-federal-guidance" target="_blank" ><u>were determined not to change local</u></a><u> guidance</u>.</p><p>“Our latest school immunization numbers validate our efforts to make vaccines more accessible,” Gov. JB Pritzker said in a prepared statement. A court order blocked federal efforts to limit the vaccines in March as a result of a federal lawsuit. Last week, the Trump administration filed an appeal last, according to the AP.</p><p>Dr. Sameer Vohra, the director of the Illinois Department of Public Health, said nearly all of the vaccines met the state’s goal of having at least 95% coverage rate. In April, Vohra told the Sun-Times that Pritzker, along with the state’s advisory immunization committee, was working to ensure residents had transparent and science-based information about vaccines as changes started to unfold on the federal level.</p><p>“I think one of the core tenants of the work in the department of public health and under Gov. Pritzker’s leadership has been to ensure that for health-related information, that it’s being credible, transparent and following gold-standard scientific processes,” Vohra said in an interview with the Sun-Times in April. “What we’ve seen from the federal government has been a push against some of those processes that many residents, healthcare providers, like myself, a general pediatrician, really counted on.”</p><p>Across the state, the vaccination rate for measles is slightly higher than in the past two school years but lower than it was during the 2020-2021 school year, according to the data.</p><p>In Chicago, the vaccination rate is 95.8%, lower than the state’s rate, according to the data. In suburban Cook County, the vaccination rate is 97% and officials there had rolled out a measles inoculation program at the start of the school year.</p><p>Pulaski County, located in the southern part of the state, was the only county that had a measles rate under 90%.</p><p>The state did see a slight decrease in the number of school children who were vaccinated against hepatitis B, according to the Illinois Department of Public Health. The rate decreased slightly from 97.2% this year from 97.3% last school year.</p><p>Last year, federal health officials stopped recommending the vaccine for newborns, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/05/cdc-vaccine-newborns-hepatitis-b-robert-kennedy-anti-vaccine-childhood" target="_blank" ><u>Illinois officials soon reaffirmed their recommendation</u></a> to continue the practice of administering the vaccine to healthy and stable infants.</p><p>The hepatitis B vaccine is typically given to infants within 24 hours of birth to prevent liver infection and chronic disease.</p><p>The state’s data also shows that there has been an increase in the number of vaccine exemptions due to religious reasons. There were 17,460 for this school year compared to 2,425 a decade ago, according to the state’s data.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/05/us-drops-the-number-of-vaccines-it-recommends-for-every-child" >Illinois won’t drop number of vaccines for children despite new federal guidance</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/05/cdc-vaccine-newborns-hepatitis-b-robert-kennedy-anti-vaccine-childhood" >Illinois officials will still recommend hepatitis B vaccine for newborns, despite new CDC guidance</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Sky waive Maddy Westbeld, Aicha Coulibaly
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> waived their 2025 second-round draft picks: Maddy Westbeld (No. 16) and Aicha Coulibaly (No. 22).</p><p>In her rookie season, Westbeld averaged 4.1 points in 13.6 minutes while shooting 39.5% from 3. Coulibaly did not join the team last season because she was recovering from an ACL injury, but had a strong training camp.</p><p>Both players were signed to rookie contracts and could still qualify for the team's two development spots. But first they must clear waivers, meaning no other team claims their contracts in the next 48 hours.</p><p>The Sky also <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/05/04/sky-waive-guard-hailey-van-lith" >waived point guard Hailey Van Lith</a> earlier Monday.</p><p>With the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/05/04/sky-sign-point-guard-natasha-cloud" >addition of veteran point guard Natasha Cloud, </a>the Sky now have 12 players on the roster (the maximum). Guard Sydney Taylor is the last training camp invitee standing. If final rosters were due today, she would make the cut, but the team could still make additional moves.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Property tax relief 'negligible' for homeowners in Bears stadium bill that passed Illinois House
entries.summary: <p>A residential property tax relief provision helped the Chicago Bears’ stadium bill <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/22/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-property-tax-relief-infrastructure-northwest-indiana" >pass the Illinois House</a> last month, but Gov. JB Pritzker's office says any help for homeowners under the proposal would be "negligible.”</p><p>That sends lawmakers back to the drawing board to hammer out so-called megaproject legislation that can keep together a diverse Democratic caucus, and keep the Bears in Illinois.</p><p>“We agree in the Senate with the House’s effort to embed property tax relief in the bill, and we are endeavoring to find the best way to do that,” state Sen. Bill Cunningham, D-Chicago, said Monday en route to Springfield, where lawmakers have until May 31 to reach a consensus. “We want to see if there is a way to make it more significant.”</p><p>Dozens of other states have such <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/24/bears-stadium-bill-jb-pritzker-illinois-senate" target="_blank" ><u>megaproject legislation</u></a> to incentivize large-scale developments, allowing companies that invest in big projects to negotiate discounted payments with local taxing bodies instead of footing their full assessed property tax bill. Pritzker has long pushed for such a measure to attract business to Illinois.</p><p>The bill championed by state Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, calls for half the proceeds from payments in lieu of taxes to be set aside for property tax rebates and relief, in an effort to help average homeowners while helping big companies.</p><p>Of that half of PILOT payments, 60% would go toward rebates for homeowners in the taxing district, and 40% would go into a statewide relief fund.</p><p>An analysis conducted by Pritzker’s office and shared with legislators found that from a hypothetical $20 million PILOT payment made for a large industrial development, a typical Illinois homeowner would see only $1.29 in relief.</p><p>Returns would shrink correspondingly for smaller developments, “resulting in negligible property tax relief for Illinoisans,” the governor’s office concluded.</p><p>Buckner acknowledged the concerns but said the framework in his bill could generate “more growth, more development, more activity across the state. And that kind of success can create a steady stream of new revenue … that we can begin directing into real property tax relief."</p><p>“Once that stream is real, we can refine it. We can revamp the program, make it more targeted,” Buckner said. “We can’t decide to do nothing because we don’t yet have the tools to do everything. I know the governor’s office, the House and the Senate agree on that. We all know we have to act. This is about taking a real, responsible first step and building from there.”</p><p>For now, it’s up to Cunningham and other leaders in his chamber to come up with a bill palatable not only for the Bears, but for Chicago Democrats who are loath to let the Bears leave the city, and progressives who are hesitant to offer any assistance to big companies while costs rise for residents.</p><p>Late add-ons to the House legislation — including tax incentives for the redevelopment of “underused railyards,” potentially paving the way for a White Sox stadium at the South Loop Amtrak yard — have also complicated negotiations.</p><p>But meaningful property tax relief for the average homeowner would go a long way toward offsetting the inescapable optics of assisting a pro sports franchise that’s valued at $8.9 billion and weighing <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/17/bears-hammond-indiana-stadium-stadium-site-meeting-kevin-warren-george-mccaskey-thomas-mcdermott-nfl-roger-goodell" target="_blank" ><u>a sweetheart deal from Indiana lawmakers</u></a>.</p><p>“A megaproject bill is not just for the Bears,” said Cunningham, who said he is optimistic that an agreement can be reached within the next few weeks. “We are hoping that it triggers other development projects. If there are other development projects, there could be more money going to property tax relief.</p><p>“It’s just difficult to determine what number we can project. We’re estimating the amount of money that would go into a property tax relief pool, because we don’t know ultimately how many megaprojects there are going to be.”</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Bulls find their new front-office leader in Bryson Graham
entries.summary: <p>The Bulls haven’t won a playoff series since 2015.</p><p>They haven’t had a No. 1 overall draft pick since hitting on Derrick Rose in 2008.</p><p>The last time they even had a winning record was in 2021-22, when they went 46-36. It was the only time in the last decade they posted a better-than-.500 record.</p><p>So Bryson Graham has a lot of work to do.</p><p>After firing executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley last month, the Bulls began rebuilding the front office by landing Graham — most recently the Hawks’ senior VP of basketball operations — in somewhat of a surprising move.</p><p>The momentum in the last few weeks seemed to be tilting toward Timberwolves GM — and former do-everything Bulls employee — Matt Lloyd, but Graham reportedly stole the show in the in-person interview process and got the job Monday.</p><p>One Bulls source stressed it had less to do with what the other candidates were lacking and more to do with how Graham wowed the hiring group throughout the process. The same source said that Graham has full autonomy to build out the front office how he sees fit and that finances won’t be an obstacle.</p><p>Naming Graham executive VP of basketball operations is only the first step.</p><p>‘‘Bryson is an elite talent evaluator who has earned tremendous respect across the league, and that stood out immediately during our process,’’ Bulls president Michael Reinsdorf said in a statement. ‘‘He has worked his way up through basketball operations from the ground level, and that experience has given him a deep understanding of how to build and sustain a successful organization.</p><p>‘‘He is an effective communicator, a disciplined and thoughtful decision-maker and someone who truly connects with players and people. He understands today’s league, today’s players and what it takes to develop talent and build a winning culture. Just as important, Bryson is committed to building a high-level group around him. He knows what he does well, and he is focused on surrounding that with strong leadership across strategy, scouting and player development. This is an important step for our organization. We know there is work ahead, but we are confident in Bryson’s ability to lead, build and move us forward.’’</p><p>Graham comes to the Bulls on a bit of a hot streak as far as front offices go. During his time with the Hawks, he completely overhauled the front office and its philosophy.</p><p>‘‘I am incredibly honored to join the Chicago Bulls organization,’’ Graham said in a statement. ‘‘This is one of the most storied franchises in the history of professional basketball, and I feel a tremendous sense of responsibility to deliver results for this city and these fans. My entire career has been built on the belief that sustained success starts with finding the right players and developing an all-around impactful culture. I want to thank Jerry and Michael Reinsdorf and the entire Bulls organization for presenting me with this opportunity. I am ready to get to work.’’</p><p>The good news for Graham is that he will inherit an organization that might feel broken but actually has a lot of solid parts and a foundation that could be built up quickly.</p><p>Forward Matas Buzelis appears to be a rising star, and guard Josh Giddey has shown flashes of being a solid piece. Graham also will have two picks among the top 15 of a loaded NBA Draft next month and, perhaps most important, has a league-high $58 million in salary-cap space this summer.</p><bsp-list-loadmore
